sections.introduction: Transformer based Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated consistent performance gains with increasing model size [ 13 , 32 ] . However, training these models at scale is resource-intensive: a mixed-precision training run requires approximately 20 bytes of memory per parameter, roughly 6 floating-point operations per parameter per token, and substantial inter-device communication [ 23 , 30 ] . The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism, in which only a fraction of the model parameters get sparsely activated for each token, has emerged as a promising approach to achieving dense-models’ performance at a reduced computational cost with savings proportional to the sparsity factor [ 31 , 8 ] . State-of-the-art models such as Mixtral [ 12 ] , DeepSeek [ 7 ] , Qwen [ 27 ] , and Kimi [ 15 ] have adopted MoE to deliver superior performance at substantially reduced training cost. Despite these advantages, MoE training presents its own unique challenges. Relative to a parameter-matched dense model, MoE training introduces computational load imbalance across devices, an elevated memory footprint from storing both model parameters and transient activation tensors, and high inter-device communication volume under expert parallelism — the most widely used MoE distribution method [ 18 , 29 ] . Emerging MoE architectures with fine-grained experts such as DeepSeek-MoE [ 5 ] , exacerbate these challenges further: they produce many tall-and-skinny GEMMs with poor hardware utilization, inflate activation memory, and require all-to-all collectives involving a large number of participating processes. These difficulties compound when training on shared HPC platforms which are primarily designed for modeling and simulation workloads and feature non-uniform communication fabrics across GPU nodes. Prior work such as X-MoE [ 3 ] has demonstrated that communication overhead becomes a dominant performance bottleneck in exactly this setting, yet no holistic framework exists for systematically characterizing, quantifying, and mitigating these inefficiencies across both model and platform architecture. In this work, we develop mathematical models to quantify memory, compute, and communication requirements for diverse MoE architectures under different distributed training strategies and their combinations, parameterized by empirically measured platform characteristics — including memory capacity, GPU throughput, and network bandwidth. We validate these models through comprehensive experimental training runs using frameworks including DeepSpeed-TED [ 19 ] , DeepSpeed-MoE [ 29 ] , and X-MoE [ 3 ] on the Frontier supercomputer [ 2 ] . Using our resource modeling framework alongside experimental profiling, we identify prominent sources of missed performance: (i) all-to-all latency at scale caused by expert parallelism over non-uniform interconnects; (ii) load imbalance from skewed expert assignment, resulting in low GPU utilization during early stages, and, in case of no external mechanisms, throughout the majority of training; and (iii) a lack of system-aware hybrid parallelization strategies. We then develop targeted solutions for each.
sections.discussion: We presented Piper, a holistic MoE training framework that co-designs distributed training strategy through mathematical resource modeling, empirical micro-benchmarking, and platform-aware performance estimation. Its central contribution is the application of pipeline parallelism on top of traditional expert-data parallelism, which proves critical in mitigating large-scale all-to-all latency. Piper achieves 2–3.6× the MFU of X-MoE on SOTA models, demonstrating that deep understanding of the target HPC platform translates directly into training throughput gains. Implemented in Python and PyTorch, Piper is portable across platforms, and its micro-benchmarking suite can characterize arbitrary HPC systems. As new MoE architectures emerge, Piper’s modular design allows incremental integration of kernel optimizations, improved schedules, and dynamic load-balancing strategies to deliver continued efficiency gains.
sections.raw_text: Piper: Efficient Large-Scale MoE Training via Resource Modeling and Pipelined Hybrid Parallelism Sajal Dash Feiyi Wang (May 2026) Abstract Frontier models increasingly adopt Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures to achieve large-model performance at reduced cost. However, training MoE models on HPC platforms is hindered by large memory footprints, frequent large-scale communication across heterogeneous networks, and severe workload imbalance. To characterize these challenges, we develop a mathematical model that quantifies memory, compute, and communication requirements for MoE configurations under various parallelization schemes, verified through micro-benchmarking, code instrumentation, and hardware profiling. Our analysis identifies performance bottlenecks: all-to-all latency at scale from expert parallelism, insufficient compute-communication overlap, low GPU utilization from imbalanced skinny GEMMs, and the absence of platform-aware hybrid parallelization strategies. To address these, we introduce Piper, a framework that leverages resource modeling to identify efficient training strategies for MoE models on target HPC platforms, applying pipeline parallelism with optimized schedules. Piper achieves 2–3.5× higher MFU than state-of-the-art frameworks such as X-MoE, and a novel all-to-all algorithm delivers 1.2X–9X bandwidth over vendor implementation. I Introduction Transformer based Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated consistent performance gains with increasing model size [ 13 , 32 ] . However, training these models at scale is resource-intensive: a mixed-precision training run requires approximately 20 bytes of memory per parameter, roughly 6 floating-point operations per parameter per token, and substantial inter-device communication [ 23 , 30 ] . The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism, in which only a fraction of the model parameters get sparsely activated for each token, has emerged as a promising approach to achieving dense-models’ performance at a reduced computational cost with savings proportional to the sparsity factor [ 31 , 8 ] . State-of-the-art models such as Mixtral [ 12 ] , DeepSeek [ 7 ] , Qwen [ 27 ] , and Kimi [ 15 ] have adopted MoE to deliver superior performance at substantially reduced training cost. Despite these advantages, MoE training presents its own unique challenges. Relative to a parameter-matched dense model, MoE training introduces computational load imbalance across devices, an elevated memory footprint from storing both model parameters and transient activation tensors, and high inter-device communication volume under expert parallelism — the most widely used MoE distribution method [ 18 , 29 ] . Emerging MoE architectures with fine-grained experts such as DeepSeek-MoE [ 5 ] , exacerbate these challenges further: they produce many tall-and-skinny GEMMs with poor hardware utilization, inflate activation memory, and require all-to-all collectives involving a large number of participating processes. These difficulties compound when training on shared HPC platforms which are primarily designed for modeling and simulation workloads and feature non-uniform communication fabrics across GPU nodes. Prior work such as X-MoE [ 3 ] has demonstrated that communication overhead becomes a dominant performance bottleneck in exactly this setting, yet no holistic framework exists for systematically characterizing, quantifying, and mitigating these inefficiencies across both model and platform architecture. In this work, we develop mathematical models to quantify memory, compute, and communication requirements for diverse MoE architectures under different distributed training strategies and their combinations, parameterized by empirically measured platform characteristics — including memory capacity, GPU throughput, and network bandwidth. We validate these models through comprehensive experimental training runs using frameworks including DeepSpeed-TED [ 19 ] , DeepSpeed-MoE [ 29 ] , and X-MoE [ 3 ] on the Frontier supercomputer [ 2 ] . Using our resource modeling framework alongside experimental profiling, we identify prominent sources of missed performance: (i) all-to-all latency at scale caused by expert parallelism over non-uniform interconnects; (ii) load imbalance from skewed expert assignment, resulting in low GPU utilization during early stages, and, in case of no external mechanisms, throughout the majority of training; and (iii) a lack of system-aware hybrid parallelization strategies. We then develop targeted solutions for each. I-A Contributions 1. Analytical and Empirical Resource Modeling: We develop a mathematical model for estimating memory, compute, and communication utilization during MoE training across a range of architectures and parallelization configurations, and empirically validate it through micro-benchmarking, code instrumentation, and hardware profiling. 2. Piper: Pipeline Parallelism for Localizing Communication: We introduce Piper, a framework that applies pipeline parallelism to intra-layer parallelization strategies in order to localize and overlap expensive collective communications, and uses the resource model to automatically identify efficient training configurations for target HPC platforms. Piper achieves 2–3.5X higher Model FLOP Utilization (MFU) compared to state-of-the-art MoE training framework, X-MoE [ 3 ] . 3. Topology-Aware All-to-All Algorithm: We design a Dragonfly-topology-aware hierarchical all-to-all algorithm that exploits the dependency structure of asynchronous point-to-point communications, groups traffic over slower inter-node and inter-cabinet links, and saturates NICs uniformly, achieving 1.5X–4X the bandwidth of vendor-provided implementations. 4. Expert Migration for Load Balancing: We develop an expert migration technique in which GPUs hosting the same layer periodically exchange experts to re-balance load, incurring an amortized overhead of less than 5% of total training time. 5. Trillion-Scale MoE Training: Using Piper and our resource modeling tool, we devise and validate training strategies for several state-of-the-art MoE models at 20–50% MFU, and demonstrate training of trillion-parameter MoE models at 20 % 20\% MFU on the Frontier supercomputer. For reference, X-MoE reported training of a 545B parameter model at 5.23 % 5.23\% MFU. II Background and Related Work We survey prior work across five dimensions relevant to this paper: MoE model architectures, distributed training frameworks and hybrid parallelism, collective communication algorithms, load balancing techniques, and pipeline parallelism. We also review HPC platform topology characteristics that motivate our system-level design choices. Table I summarizes the architectural parameters of representative state-of-the-art MoE models. II-A MoE Architectures The MoE mechanism replaces the feed-forward network (FFN) sublayer in a Transformer block [ 32 ] with a collection of expert FFNs, each receiving only the tokens routed to it by a learned gating function [ 31 ] . There are two primary architectural streams. Coarse-grained MoE. Early large-scale MoE models such as GShard [ 18 ] , Switch Transformer [ 8 ] , and the Mixtral family [ 12 ] employ a small number (typically 8–64) of large experts whose FFN dimension matches a comparably sized dense model, with top-1 or top-2 routing per token. Because individual experts frequently exceed single-GPU memory capacity, they require tensor parallelism or sharded data parallelism, substantially complicating the communication pattern. R = routed, S = shared. Fine-grained MoE. Pioneered by DeepSeek-MoE [ 5 ] and followed by Qwen3 [ 28 ] , Kimi K2 [ 16 ] , and others, this approach decomposes each expert into many smaller experts; reducing FFN dimension by a factor m m while increasing expert count proportionally and selects a larger top-K ( K ∈ [ 6 , 8 , 16 ] K\in[6,8,16] ). 16]) per token. While individual experts now fit within a single GPU without tensor splitting, fine-grained designs generate tall-and-skinny GEMMs with poor hardware utilization, inflate activation memory by factor mm m, and require all-to-all collectives spanning many GPUs. II-B Distributed Training Frameworks and Hybrid Parallelism Training large MoE models requires combining data parallelism (DP) [ 30 ] , tensor parallelism (TP) [ 22 ] , pipeline parallelism (PP) [ 20 , 21 ] , and expert parallelism (EP) [ 18 ] , where different experts are placed on different devices and tokens are routed via all-to-all collectives. DeepSpeed-MoE [ 29 ] combines expert parallelism with tensor parallelism and ZeRO memory sharding [ 30 ] , primarily targeting coarse-grained architectures. DeepSpeed-TED [ 19 ] extends this by jointly optimizing across Tensor, Expert, and Data parallelism axes. X-MoE [ 3 ] targets fine-grained expert architectures, identifying activation memory and all-to-all scope as primary bottlenecks. It introduces zero-padding for load balancing, redundancy-based communication bypassing, and sequence-sharded parallelism, successfully outperforming DeepSpeed-MoE and Tutel at hundreds-of-billions scale. However, for 500B+ models, X-MoE achieves only 5 % 5\% MFU. Tutel [ 11 ] provides efficient MoE dispatch and combine kernels with dynamic top-K routing and adaptive parallelism switching, but focuses on the dispatch kernel rather than end-to-end training strategy selection and does not cover attention-layer parallelization. A general limitation across these frameworks is the absence of platform-aware hybrid parallelism planning that jointly accounts for memory, compute, and communication constraints, a gap Piper directly addresses (Section III ). II-C Pipeline Parallelism Pipeline parallelism partitions model layers across devices using micro-batching [ 20 ] . The 1F1B schedule [ 21 ] reduces pipeline bubble fraction and peak activation memory over GPipe [ 10 ] ; interleaved variants [ 23 ] and ZB-H1/H2 schedules [ 26 ] reduce bubble overhead further. These techniques target dense models where communication occurs between layers rather than within them. Piper extends pipeline parallelism to the intra-layer axis introduced by expert parallelism, enabling computation-communication overlap within MoE layers. II-D Load Balancing Uneven token distribution across experts reduces effective GPU throughput. Common mitigations include auxiliary load-balancing losses [ 8 , 18 ] , token dropping [ 8 ] , expert-choice routing [ 34 ] , and the auxiliary-loss-free bias-adjustment strategy of DeepSeekV3 [ 7 ] . All operate at the routing level and cannot correct device-level imbalance from oblivious expert placement. Our expert migration approach (Section VI ) complements these methods by physically redistributing experts based on observed load. II-E Collective Communication Algorithms Under expert parallelism, each MoE layer incurs four all-to-all operations (two per forward pass and two per backward), making all-to-all a dominant latency contributor at scale [ 3 , 29 ] . Flat all-to-all. NCCL [ 25 ] and RCCL [ 1 ] perform direct point-to-point transfers between all process pairs, which is bandwidth-optimal under a uniform topology but performs poorly on hierarchical networks where inter-node bandwidth is significantly lower than intra-node bandwidth. Hierarchical all-to-all. Tutel [ 11 ] , FasterMoE [ 9 ] , and HetuMoE [ 24 ] use a two-phase approach, intra-node aggregation followed by reduced inter-node transfers, substantially reducing cross-node messages. However, these algorithms treat the inter-node network as homogeneous. Dragonfly topology. HPC systems such as Frontier [ 2 ] employ Dragonfly networks [ 14 ] with high-bandwidth intra-group links and sparser inter-group links. Topology-oblivious algorithms cause unnecessary contention on slower inter-group links. Our topology-aware all-to-all (Section V ) explicitly models this three-level hierarchy (intra-node, intra-group, inter-group) and coordinates asynchronous point-to-point communication to eliminate idle cycles. II-F Analytical Performance Modeling Roofline analysis [ 33 ] bounds achievable performance by arithmetic intensity and memory bandwidth, informing kernel optimization for attention and FFN layers [ 6 ] . Korthikanti et al. [ 17 ] develop analytical models for pipeline bubble and activation memory in dense Transformer training; PaLM [ 4 ] uses empirical roofline fitting to project hardware efficiency across configurations. For MoE models, interactions among expert parallelism, routing, and load imbalance introduce variables that dense-model frameworks do not capture. No prior work provides a unified model spanning memory, compute, and communication jointly across different parallelization dimensions validated on a real HPC platform, a gap we address in Section III . III Piper: A Framework for Pipelining MoE Training via Resource Modeling Piper is a framework for efficient MoE model training on HPC platforms. Its design rests on two observations. First, existing frameworks such as DeepSpeed-MoE, DeepSpeed-TED, and X-MoE, distribute all model components across large, groups of GPUs, forcing expensive collective communications (4 all-reduce for tensor parallelism, 2 all-gather for sharded data parallelism, 4 all-to-all for expert parallelism) to span many ranks simultaneously. Second, pipeline parallelism, which is the standard tool for bounding communication group size in dense model training [ 23 ] , has not been applied to MoE training due to the added complexity of intra-layer expert parallelism. Piper closes this gap by composing pipeline parallelism with expert parallelism. It organizes P P GPUs into a P ​ P × E ​ P PP\times EP device mesh: P ​ P PP pipeline stages, each staffed by E ​ P EP GPUs that handle one partition of experts for L / P ​ P L/PP layers via expert-data parallelism. Confining expert-parallel communication to a small, topologically local group of GPUs, ideally within a single node or a single-hop Rosetta switch group on Frontier allows Piper to exploit fast intra-node interconnects and avoid the high latency of large-scale all-to-all collectives. We expand on Tutel [ 11 ] to support the underlying expert-parallelism. Piper consists of four components, described in turn: (i) an analytical resource model that estimates memory, compute, and communication for any ( P ​ P , E ​ P ) (PP,EP) configuration and a model architecture (Section III-A 1 ); (ii) a micro-benchmarking suite that measures platform-specific bandwidth and throughput to parameterize the model (Section IV-A ); (iii) a performance estimator that scores valid configurations by predicted MFU (Section IV-C ); and (iv) a pipelined training executor that implements the selected strategy with an efficient 1F1B schedule (Section VII ). Figure 1 : Piper framework for efficient MoE training At the core of our framework is Pipeline Parallelism on top of expert parallelism (Figure 2 ). We use Tutel for facilitating expert-parallelism. Piper framework has a resource modeling component, a micro-benchmarking suit, a performance estimator, and a pipelined training tool that partitions MoE models across layers. Figure 2 : Pipeline Parallelism on Expert Parallelism III-A Resource Modeling for MoE Training Training large MoE models faces two coupled resource constraints: (i) memory pressure from model parameters, optimizer states, and expert activations, and (ii) communication latency from all-to-all collectives under expert parallelism. We develop analytical models for both, parameterized by the notation in Table II , and use them to prune the ( P ​ P , E ​ P ) (PP,EP) search space to configurations that are memory-feasible and to rank feasible configurations by predicted throughput. III-D Pipelined Training Execution Among valid configurations, Piper ranks them by estimated MFU. Then it interfaces PyTorch Distributed Pipeline Parallelism with expert-parallel or any other hybrid intra-node distribution library such as Tutel. We instrumented 1F1B schedule and installed synchronization mechanisms among expert-parallel group members. We expanded Tutel and PyTorch’s pipeline parallelism mechanism to work with each other under a two-dimensional parallelization (three, counting external data parallelism). IV Performance Modeling through Micro-benchmarking Resource modeling gives us a realistic expectation regarding viable distributed training strategies in terms of number of nodes, degrees of expert and pipeline data parallelism, whether to adopt memory saving techniques such as checkpoint activation, offloading, etc. This can be done largely statically by the mathematical formulas we developed in Section III-A and considering system properties such as HMB memory. Once we find candidate strategies to train our model without running into out of resource (e.g., OOM) error. To estimate the model flops utilization or MFU for each of these strategies, we need to micro-benchmark the HPC platform for various phases of the MoE training workflow. With pipelined expert-data parallelism, the typical flow is ( a ​ t ​ t ​ n → r ​ o ​ u ​ t ​ i ​ n ​ g → d ​ i ​ s ​ p ​ a ​ t ​ c ​ h ​ _ ​ a ​ 2 ​ a → e ​ x ​ p ​ e ​ r ​ t → c ​ o ​ m ​ b ​ i ​ n ​ e ​ _ ​ a ​ 2 ​ a ) × l → P ​ 2 ​ P \Big(attn\rightarrow routing\rightarrow dispatch\_a2a\rightarrow expert\rightarrow combine\_a2a\Big)_{\times l}\rightarrow P2P So, we need to run micro-benchmarking on computational performance and communication latency. IV-A Micro-benchmarking Computation Every transformer layer has two major components, non-expert attention module, and multiple Feed Forward Network (FFN) experts. In an expert-data parallel setting, we need to distribute the experts equally across the g g gpus in a node and replicate the attention module in a data-parallel way across all g g gpus. Each GPU needs to host and process one attention module and E / g E/g experts. We start with separate optimization of these two parts and identify the suitable kernels optimized for each part. IV-A 1 Attention Performance For attention part, different models have different model dimension, and the flash-attention kernel is optimized for only a fixed set of head-dimension. We benchmark a single GPU to identify the best performing head-dimension. We need to choose a set of best throughput generation < b a t c h _ s i z e , h e a d _ d i m e n s i o n > <batch\_size,head\_dimension> tuples. Figure 3 shows our measure performances for various MoE model architectures. Figure 3 : Achievable throughput for various models for different sequence lengths. IV-A 2 Expert Performance Similarly, for the FFN part, which mostly requires GEMM operations, we need to identify the set of (num_tokens, batch_size, expert_dimension) tuples that gives us the best throughput. For the fine-grained experts where GEMM between many tall and skinny matrices are involved, the search becomes more involved (Figure 4 ). Then, from these two sets, we need to identify the tuples with agreeing b ​ a ​ t ​ c ​ h ​ _ ​ s ​ i ​ z ​ e batch\_size . Choosing this batch_size becomes a critical choice, especially in the context of pipeline parallelism since choosing the right number of micro batch size, and right value for micro batch size will determine pipeline bubble and computation efficiency. Figure 4 : MoE GEMM Performance IV-B Micro-benchmarking Communication In our framework, we incur two types of communication, all-to-all within an expert-parallel group and send-recv between two pipeline stages. Since, between stage send-recv communication happens following a synchronization across the expert parallel group, there are EP concurrent P2P communications between two stages. For benchmarking all-to-all bandwidth, we vary the number of GPUs from 2 to 64 spanning 1-8 nodes, for various message sizes (Figure 5 ). Figure 5 : Benchmarking All-to-all bandwidth for various message sizes. Bandwidth drops significantly as soon as all-to-all involves inter-node communicaiton IV-C MFU Estimation Let t compute t_{\mathrm{compute}} and t comm t_{\mathrm{comm}} be the attention + expert and A2A + P2P totals summed over the full step. Then: MFU = ℱ model π ^ eff ⋅ G ⋅ t compute ⏟ hardware efficiency × t compute t step ⏟ compute fraction , t compute t step = 1 − b − t comm t step \mathrm{MFU}=\underbrace{\frac{\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{model}}}{\hat{\pi}_{\mathrm{eff}}\cdot G\cdot t_{\mathrm{compute}}}}_{\text{hardware efficiency}}\times\underbrace{\frac{t_{\mathrm{compute}}}{t_{\mathrm{step}}}}_{\text{compute fraction}},\qquad\frac{t_{\mathrm{compute}}}{t_{\mathrm{step}}}=1-b-\frac{t_{\mathrm{comm}}}{t_{\mathrm{step}}} (12) V HALO: Hierarchical Affinity-aware Locality-Optimized All-to-All Our algorithm pursues five design goals. It saturates all four NICs on a single node during inter-node communication, and identifies the three phases of communication along with their dependency structure in order to maximize concurrency across phases. At the intra-node level, it enforces maximum locality by exploiting GPU-to-NIC affinity. At the inter-node level, it leverages the Dragonfly grouping structure by treating the four nodes connected to a common Rosetta switch as a single communication locality domain. Finally, when possible, it constrains node allocation to within the same rack entirely, avoiding the slowest inter-rack communication links. V-A Communication Group Construction We assign GPUs sharing a NIC to the same inter-node communicator, ensuring that inter-node traffic from a given GPU is always injected through its affinitized NIC. This prevents NIC contention and allows all four NICs on a node to operate at full bandwidth simultaneously. Phase III depends on the completion of Phase II, as it redistributes the received remote data within the node. We exploit the Phase I independence by launching it concurrently with Phase II or Phase III, hiding a significant portion of intra-node communication latency behind inter-node transfers. Figure 7 demonstrates our three phases and their overlapping strategies. Figure 7 : Three phases of our HALO all-to-all algorithm. Phase I can happen concurrently with either of Phase II and Phase III, but Phase III needs to wait for Phase II to complete. V-C Rack-Aware Node Allocation When the job scheduler permits, HALO constrains node allocation to within a single rack to eliminate inter-rack traffic, which traverses the slowest links in the Dragonfly topology. Within a rack, nodes are further grouped by Rosetta switch affinity: the four nodes sharing a switch form a single locality domain, and inter-group communication is batched across this boundary to minimize contention on inter-switch links. V-D Comparison against torch.dist.all_to_all We compare HALO against torch.dist.all_to_all backed by RCCL across varying node counts and message sizes (Figure 8 ). HALO achieves 1.1 × \times –9 × \times lower latency for configurations of 16 nodes or more. The crossover at 16 nodes reflects the threshold at which inter-rack communication becomes dominant under the flat RCCL implementation — precisely the regime where HALO’s rack-aware grouping and NIC saturation strategy yield the largest gains. At smaller scales, where all GPUs fit within a single switch group, both algorithms perform comparably since single-hop communication already saturates available bandwidth. Figure 8 : Latency comparison of Neighborhood-all_to_all algorithm against the RCCL based torch.dist.all_to_all algorithm. VI Load Balancing through Expert Migration The load across GPUs emerging from router’s expert selection can vary over training period. Initially, all experts are preferred by the router equally; however small random difference can make some experts more favorable. These favored experts get more tokens, and consequently more gradient updates. This creates a positive feedback loop where a subset of experts become more capable than others, and end up getting more tokens consistently. This causes expert collapse. In the middle part of the training, experts become specialized in different types of input. The load distribution stabilizes, and different experts develop distinct activation patterns across the feature space. Towards the end, the specialization becomes more concrete. Some experts remain underutilized. Unless there is an auxiliary load-balancing loss or other load balancing mechanism in place, expert workload remains skewed. So, a static expert parallelism at the beginning of the training cannot ensure that all the GPUs within a layer are receiving balanced workload. While load balancing loss helps with load balancing in the middle stages of the training, it still suffers from prolonged period of load imbalance. Figure 9 shows expert-routing distribution in different layer becomes balanced after almost one billion token consumption. Existing MoE training methods are averse to expert re-assignment for load balancing due to the perceived overhead of expert migration. Under existing distribution techniques that distribute experts among many nodes through slow Ethernet, this caution is justified. However, when we successfully utilize pipeline parallelism to localize the experts from the same layer to a small number of GPUs connected via fast link (NVLink or Infinity Fabric), we should reconsider the dynamic load balancing. In the absence of load balancing loss, the expert demands are still skewed, but load across the GPUs can be balanced. Even with the expert collapse, the final model performance does not suffer. Figure 9 : Expert load distribution during the training process. The model has a 350M base model, with 16 experts and expert frequency 0.5. VI-A Dynamic Expert Migration We extend the router class to maintain token distribution throughout the training. An external scheduler (could be intermittent interrupt in the training loop) inspects the growing load imbalance, and whenever it crosses a pre-determined threshold, it can run our migration algorithm to identify the minimal number of intra-group (expert-parallel group) migrations of experts among GPUs. The cost of migration will be the cost of an all-to-all in the expert parallel group where individual expert’s migration cost is the latency of moving 48 ​ d m ​ o ​ d ​ e ​ l ​ d f ​ f ​ n 48d_{model}d_{ffn} ( 3 ​ d m ​ o ​ d ​ e ​ l ​ d f ​ f ​ n 3d_{model}d_{ffn} expert parameters, and each parameter needs 16 Bytes for master copy, optimizer states, and gradients). For example to train a model (super model with 545B parameters), we computed the required memory for various node count with different distribution strategies (Figure 10 ). Figure 10 : Identifying viable training strategies for a model by estimating its per-GPU memory requirement. Here, we investigate training strategy of a 615 B parameter model. And, just like this analysis suggests, we can train this model with at least 64 nodes. VII-A Trroughput Ceiling : Training a Single Layer in a Node Every transformer layer has two major components, non-expert attention module, and multiple Feed Forward Network (FFN) experts. In an expert-data parallel setting, we need to distribute the experts equally across the G gpus in a node and replicate the attention module in a data-parallel way across all G gpus. Each GPU needs to host and process one attention module and E G \dfrac{E}{G} experts. After getting the suitable configuration, we train a single layer of the SOTA models on a single Frontier node using expert-parallelism and observe their training performance (Figure 11 ). We were able to fit a single layer to a single node for each of the models we tried. With memory constraint under pipelined expert-parallelism and pipeline bubble under various schedules, this sets up the upper limit of training performance of any model using Piper. Figure 11 : Training efficiency of a single layer of the SOTA models. VII-B Training the Full Model With our resource modeling, we identified valid parallelization strategies for various SOTA models and train them using their original size. We selectively use activation checkpointing to fit the model into a small number of GPUs while minimizing the impact on the MFU (Figure 12 ). From Table 12 , we observe that training models with traditional large experts achieves best performance while the fine-grained experts achieve lower performance. Figure 12 : Training throughput of SOTA MoE models using Piper (sequence length 4096). Hatching denotes activation checkpointing. VII-C Comparison Against Other MoE Training Frameworks We compare Piper’s training throughput for different model sizes against state of the art training frameworks such as Tutel, DeepSpeed MoE, DeepSpeed TED, and most prominent X-MoE. Since X-MoE is the leading open-source framework for fine-grained experts training, we compare Piper against these tools using fine-grained MoEs. Our framework Piper can train these models with only a fraction of their requirement at 2-3.6X throughput (Figure 13 ). Figure 13 : Throughput comparison against SOTA MoE training frameworks. Piper can train the small, medium, large, and super model using 8, 32, 80, and 512 MI250X GPUS instead of 256 and 1024 GPUs used by X-MoE. VII-D Training Trillion(s) Parameter Models We take a dense model [ d m ​ o ​ d ​ e ​ l = 5120 , d f ​ f ​ n = 20480 , L = 32 , k = 2 ] [d_{model}=5120,d_{ffn}=20480,L=32,k=2] (10 Billion parameters, let’s call it M10B) and scale out its parameter count by scaling the number of experts ( E E ). We start with 16 experts on 8 nodes and then scale E E proportionally. We use 128 experts on 64 nodes (512 GPUs), and 256 experts on 128 nodes (1024 GPUs). With this scaling, we can train a 862 Billion parameter model using 512 GPUs at 39.38 TFLOPs, and a 1.7 Trillion parameter model using 1024 GPUs at 33 TFLOPS. This is a form of weak scaling, and the scaling efficiency is 73% from 64 GPUs to 1024 GPUs (Figure 14 ). Figure 14 : Scaling M10B models with experts. VIII Conclusion and Discussion We presented Piper, a holistic MoE training framework that co-designs distributed training strategy through mathematical resource modeling, empirical micro-benchmarking, and platform-aware performance estimation. Its central contribution is the application of pipeline parallelism on top of traditional expert-data parallelism, which proves critical in mitigating large-scale all-to-all latency. Piper achieves 2–3.6× the MFU of X-MoE on SOTA models, demonstrating that deep understanding of the target HPC platform translates directly into training throughput gains. Implemented in Python and PyTorch, Piper is portable across platforms, and its micro-benchmarking suite can characterize arbitrary HPC systems. As new MoE architectures emerge, Piper’s modular design allows incremental integration of kernel optimizations, improved schedules, and dynamic load-balancing strategies to deliver continued efficiency gains. full_text:
TITLE: Piper: Efficient Large-Scale MoE Training via Resource Modeling and Pipelined Hybrid Parallelism

ABSTRACT: Frontier models increasingly adopt Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures to achieve large-model performance at reduced cost. However, training MoE models on HPC platforms is hindered by large memory footprints, frequent large-scale communication across heterogeneous networks, and severe workload imbalance. To characterize these challenges, we develop a mathematical model that quantifies memory, compute, and communication requirements for MoE configurations under various parallelization schemes, verified through micro-benchmarking, code instrumentation, and hardware profiling. Our analysis identifies performance bottlenecks: all-to-all latency at scale from expert parallelism, insufficient compute-communication overlap, low GPU utilization from imbalanced skinny GEMMs, and the absence of platform-aware hybrid parallelization strategies. To address these, we introduce Piper, a framework that leverages resource modeling to identify efficient training strategies for MoE models on target HPC platforms, applying pipeline parallelism with optimized schedules. Piper achieves 2-3.5X higher MFU than state-of-the-art frameworks such as X-MoE, and a novel all-to-all algorithm delivers 1.2-9X bandwidth over vendor implementation.

INTRODUCTION: Transformer based Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated consistent performance gains with increasing model size [ 13 , 32 ] . However, training these models at scale is resource-intensive: a mixed-precision training run requires approximately 20 bytes of memory per parameter, roughly 6 floating-point operations per parameter per token, and substantial inter-device communication [ 23 , 30 ] . The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism, in which only a fraction of the model parameters get sparsely activated for each token, has emerged as a promising approach to achieving dense-models’ performance at a reduced computational cost with savings proportional to the sparsity factor [ 31 , 8 ] . State-of-the-art models such as Mixtral [ 12 ] , DeepSeek [ 7 ] , Qwen [ 27 ] , and Kimi [ 15 ] have adopted MoE to deliver superior performance at substantially reduced training cost. Despite these advantages, MoE training presents its own unique challenges. Relative to a parameter-matched dense model, MoE training introduces computational load imbalance across devices, an elevated memory footprint from storing both model parameters and transient activation tensors, and high inter-device communication volume under expert parallelism — the most widely used MoE distribution method [ 18 , 29 ] . Emerging MoE architectures with fine-grained experts such as DeepSeek-MoE [ 5 ] , exacerbate these challenges further: they produce many tall-and-skinny GEMMs with poor hardware utilization, inflate activation memory, and require all-to-all collectives involving a large number of participating processes. These difficulties compound when training on shared HPC platforms which are primarily designed for modeling and simulation workloads and feature non-uniform communication fabrics across GPU nodes. Prior work such as X-MoE [ 3 ] has demonstrated that communication overhead becomes a dominant performance bottleneck in exactly this setting, yet no holistic framework exists for systematically characterizing, quantifying, and mitigating these inefficiencies across both model and platform architecture. In this work, we develop mathematical models to quantify memory, compute, and communication requirements for diverse MoE architectures under different distributed training strategies and their combinations, parameterized by empirically measured platform characteristics — including memory capacity, GPU throughput, and network bandwidth. We validate these models through comprehensive experimental training runs using frameworks including DeepSpeed-TED [ 19 ] , DeepSpeed-MoE [ 29 ] , and X-MoE [ 3 ] on the Frontier supercomputer [ 2 ] . Using our resource modeling framework alongside experimental profiling, we identify prominent sources of missed performance: (i) all-to-all latency at scale caused by expert parallelism over non-uniform interconnects; (ii) load imbalance from skewed expert assignment, resulting in low GPU utilization during early stages, and, in case of no external mechanisms, throughout the majority of training; and (iii) a lack of system-aware hybrid parallelization strategies. We then develop targeted solutions for each.

DISCUSSION: We presented Piper, a holistic MoE training framework that co-designs distributed training strategy through mathematical resource modeling, empirical micro-benchmarking, and platform-aware performance estimation. Its central contribution is the application of pipeline parallelism on top of traditional expert-data parallelism, which proves critical in mitigating large-scale all-to-all latency. Piper achieves 2–3.6× the MFU of X-MoE on SOTA models, demonstrating that deep understanding of the target HPC platform translates directly into training throughput gains. Implemented in Python and PyTorch, Piper is portable across platforms, and its micro-benchmarking suite can characterize arbitrary HPC systems. As new MoE architectures emerge, Piper’s modular design allows incremental integration of kernel optimizations, improved schedules, and dynamic load-balancing strategies to deliver continued efficiency gains.

sections.introduction: Windows Component Object Model (COM) services constitute a vast attack surface for local privilege escalation. Hundreds of privileged COM classes run in Windows service processes with elevated privileges, and many are accessible to authenticated users through Remote Procedure Call (RPC) ( microsoft2024com ; gu2022comrace ) . Race conditions in these services arise when concurrent RPC threads access shared object state without synchronization, producing use-after-free and double-free vulnerabilities that yield arbitrary code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service ( gu2022comrace ; upadhyay2023navigating ) . These vulnerabilities are prevalent across Windows installations yet remain difficult to detect. Analysis requires reasoning about thread interleavings, object lifetimes, and locking protocols simultaneously across functions that optimizing compilers strip of all type and symbol information. Existing approaches to detecting race conditions in COM binaries face fundamental limitations. Coverage-guided fuzzers lack control over thread scheduling to trigger specific interleavings ( jeong2019razzer ) . Static analyzers such as COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) rely on exhaustive pair enumeration that produces false positives from incorrect alias results and unmodeled input conditions, achieving only 0.299 F1 in our evaluation ( § 6.5 ). Structural assumptions about register usage and vtable layouts further miss vulnerabilities across diverse binary patterns. Manual reverse engineering requires decompiling each entry function, tracing shared-state accesses, and checking locking discipline across many functions, which does not scale to production COM services. In recent years, Large language models (LLMs) offer a complementary capability. LLMs demonstrate strong semantic reasoning over decompiled code, recovering variable names, data structures, and types from binaries ( xie2024resym ; xu2025gennm ) . Agentic systems extend this by scaffolding LLM reasoning with external tool invocation and iterative validation ( yao2022react ; yang2024swe ; wang2024openhands ) . Pioneering work demonstrates that LLM agents can discover real-world vulnerabilities that elude traditional fuzzers ( projectzero2024naptime ; projectzero2024bigsleep ) . The Model Context Protocol (MCP) ( anthropic2024mcp ) standardizes tool interfaces, enabling agents such as Codex ( codex2025 ) and Claude Code ( Cc ) ( claudecode2026 ) to invoke binary analysis tools autonomously. The missing piece is a structured pipeline that channels LLM reasoning toward vulnerability-specific analysis patterns and extends from discovery through automated proof-of-concept (PoC) generation. We present Slyp , the first end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and generating debugger-verified PoC programs. Slyp provides three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration, COM inspection, and debugging. Together they cover decompilation and virtual dispatch resolution, interface metadata and activation templates, as well as live PoC compilation, execution, and crash capture. The agent follows a standard ReAct loop ( yao2022react ) with engineering features purpose-built for long-horizon binary analysis. These include pre-compaction checkpointing that preserves cross-function findings across context compression and task verification that prevents premature termination. The pipeline is model-agnostic and works across GPT and Claude models without modification. We evaluate Slyp on discovery and PoC generation against production coding agents ( Codex and Cc ) across six backbone models with identical task prompts. On a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases, Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 (GPT-5.4), while production agents with default minimal binary analysis tools reach 0.910 ( Codex , GPT-5.4) and 0.724 ( Cc , Opus 4.6). Augmenting production agents with all binary exploration tools narrows but does not close the gap, with Slyp still leading on every tested model by up to +0.208 F1. Moreover, Slyp also outperforms COMRace++ (our enhanced reproduction of the state-of-the-art static analyzer COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) ) by 3.3 × \times (0.973 vs. 0.299). On PoC generation, production agents without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools solve essentially no cases, while the best Slyp configuration solves 67.5% (27/40) and the four top configurations collectively solve 34 of 40. Deployed on production Windows services, Slyp has discovered 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across 9 COM services. MSRC has confirmed all 28 vulnerabilities with 16 CVEs and awarded $140,000 in bounties. This paper makes the following contributions: • We build three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration ( § 4.3 ), COM inspection ( § 4.4 ), and dynamic debugging ( § 4.5 ) that integrate with any MCP-compatible agent and are the primary contributors to both vulnerability discovery and PoC generation. • We design and implement Slyp , an end-to-end agentic pipeline that combines a ReAct loop with engineering features for long-horizon binary analysis, outperforming production coding agents on every tested model for vulnerability discovery ( § 6.3 ) and PoC generation ( § 6.4 ). While this paper primarily focuses on Windows COM binaries, Slyp can be easily generalized to other commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries. • We construct a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases with manually-verified ground-truth labels, including 8 independently discovered 1-day CVEs for circularity-free validation, and provide agent trajectory artifacts across three scaffolds and six backbone models ( § 6 ). • We demonstrate practical impact through the discovery of 28 zero-day vulnerabilities across 9 production Windows services, all confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs assigned and bounties totaling $140,000 ( § 6.2 ).
sections.discussion: Domain-Specific Knowledge Requirements. LLMs lack comprehensive knowledge of platform-specific APIs and synchronization primitives, impacting analysis accuracy for race conditions involving critical sections, locks, and reference counting semantics. We address this through in-context learning ( brown2020language ; wei2022chain ) by incorporating detailed explanations of synchronization APIs, locking patterns, and COM lifecycle management into task prompts. This proves effective, as evidenced by 28 confirmed vulnerabilities discovered in production Windows services. A natural next step is domain-specific fine-tuning or knowledge augmentation to reduce prompt-engineering overhead ( lyu2024knowtuning ; meng2022locating ) . Decompilation and Symbols. Slyp leverages official Microsoft debug symbols to produce high-quality pseudocode via IDA Pro’s Hex-Rays decompiler. Like other systems ( han2023queryx ) , mis-inferred types or control flow can hide or fabricate bug patterns even with symbols. Slyp mitigates this through multiple decompilation views, LLM reasoning that tolerates artifacts, and vtable resolution that provides ground-truth virtual dispatch targets ( xie2024resym ; shang2024far ; jiang2025beyond ) . Context Window Scaling. Stage 1 discovery completes within current 400K–1M+ token context windows, but Stage 2 PoC generation involves iterative compile-execute-debug cycles that consume 7.7–11.4M tokens per case on the strongest configurations ( Appendix D ). Slyp ’s pre-compaction checkpointing ( § 4.2 ) manages this overhead, contributing to 22–61% token reductions on Claude backbones relative to the production agent. Larger or more complex targets, such as analyzing hundreds of COM objects or tracing cross-binary dependencies, would exceed current limits. Recent work confirms that increasing context length degrades LLM performance even with sufficient retrieval capacity ( du2025context ; hong2025context ) , motivating structured management approaches that decouple reasoning depth from the raw token budget available to the model. Model Scaling and Task Decomposition. The RQ2 results reveal an inverse relationship between model capability and scaffold benefit, with the largest gap on Haiku 4.5 (+0.208 F1) and smallest on GPT-5.3-codex (+0.003). Task verification compensates for weaker reasoning by preventing premature termination, while stronger models partially self-organize without explicit scaffolding. This matters for cost-sensitive deployment, as Slyp makes smaller models viable for vulnerability reasoning that would otherwise require frontier-class capabilities, and the complementarity analysis ( § 6.4 ) suggests that ensembling across cost tiers can increase coverage beyond any single configuration. Generalization and Limitations. Adapting Slyp to a different vulnerability class or platform requires modifying the task prompt and potentially adding new MCP tools, though the binary exploration server and agent architecture (pre-compaction checkpointing, task verification) are fully reusable. Limitations include dependence on decompiler quality, non-determinism in LLM reasoning (addressed via the best-of-3 protocol), and the inherent difficulty of triggering race conditions due to timing-sensitive crash behavior. The six unsolved PoC cases involve complicated threading object acquisitions where agents must coordinate multiple concurrent operations to trigger the race condition.
sections.limitations: COMRace++ ’s limited base-configuration performance (0.225 F1, 0.500 recall, and 32 missed vulnerable functions out of 64) stems from four structural limitations that persist despite our engineering enhancements. Field-scoped taint origin. COMRace++ tracks shared state exclusively through this -relative field offsets, as taint propagation originates from the this pointer passed via rcx . Race conditions arising from global variables or other non-instance shared memory fall outside this taint scope entirely. C14 exemplifies this gap. All eight vulnerable functions involve unsynchronized access to global variables, and COMRace++ achieves F1 = 0 across all three configurations ( Table 7 ). Slyp detects all eight with every backbone model except Haiku 4.5, as LLM-based reasoning naturally considers all forms of shared mutable state regardless of provenance. WinRT produce<T,I> wrapper. WinRT’s produce<T,I> template wraps every interface method with a branchless null-safe this -pointer adjustment: lea rax,[rcx-8]; neg rcx; sbb rcx,rcx; and rcx,rax . The expression (a1 - 8) & -(int64)(a1 != 0) adjusts the interface pointer back to the implementation object when non-null. The taint engine’s binary operation handler for And can partially recover the (’this’, -8) value from the lea operand, but recursive member analysis expects rcx == (’this’, 0) . A value of (’this’, -8) does not satisfy this check, so the callee’s field accesses are unattributed. Different produce<> instantiations use different offsets ( this-8 , this-0x10 , this-0x18 ) depending on the interface’s position in the inheritance hierarchy. Allowing arbitrary offsets and arithmetic operations on pointer values as valid this aliases without type information would create false field-offset mappings across unrelated interfaces and sharply raise the false-positive rate. Indirect field access through struct construction. A further class of false negatives occurs when a method embeds the this pointer into a locally constructed struct and passes that struct to a transaction or callback mechanism. When this is stored into a stack-allocated struct ( v12[1] = this ) and the struct’s address is passed to a callee via rcx , the taint tracker sees rcx = (’rsp’, 0x40) , a stack pointer rather than a this -derived value. Recovering the relationship requires points-to analysis: tracking which memory locations contain pointers to which objects. The current engine tracks (’base’, offset) tuples through registers and direct stack slots but does not model struct-embedded pointer indirection. Statically-linked internal helpers. Statically-linked internal helper functions ( e . g . std::wstring constructors compiled into the binary) may be recovered only partially by lightweight static analysis. When the callee’s internal CFG or IR is incomplete, field accesses performed inside the helper can be missed during callee analysis. This primarily affects Write detection in our implementation, because confirming a write requires recoverable internal basic blocks that the fast CFG recovery does not produce, whereas Read accesses can still be conservatively approximated.
sections.conclusion: We present Slyp , the first end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and generating debugger-verified PoC programs without source code access. Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 on 20 COM objects, while production coding agents with default minimal tools reach 0.724–0.910, and state-of-the-art static analyzer COMRace++ achieves only 0.299. The specialized toolsets also benefit existing production agents, with augmenting them from minimal to full binary exploration improving discovery F1 by up to 0.214 and adding COM inspection with dynamic debugging increasing verified PoC cases by 1.7–2.0 × \times . On production Windows services, Slyp discovers 28 zero-day vulnerabilities across 9 COM services, confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs assigned and $140,000 in bounties.

Reproduction methodology. We reproduce COMRace using IDA Python and Angr ( angrsite ) , implementing virtual function recovery, taint propagation, and field usage detection for read / write / free operations. The original vtable recovery algorithm assumes vtable addresses are loaded exclusively via the rcx register. Our dataset contains diverse vtable loading patterns across multiple registers, rendering the original assumptions inapplicable. Since vulnerability detection depends entirely on successful virtual function recovery, the vanilla implementation fails completely on our dataset. We extend the recovery logic to handle diverse register usage patterns and additional edge cases in production binaries, yielding COMRace++ . Validation. We validate COMRace++ against the original COMRace datasets, confirming that it produces field usage patterns consistent with the original paper and detects the vulnerabilities reported in that work.

Algorithm 2 presents the virtual function call recovery procedure, which resolves indirect calls through vtable pointers to their concrete target functions. This recovery is partial because backward object-provenance tracing may fail to resolve a vtable when the definition chain is broken by indirect assignments, cross-module calls, or compiler-driven register reuse. Algorithm Overview. The algorithm processes all call instructions in a function (Lines 3 – 17 ), filtering for virtual calls and resolving each to concrete function targets. The core workflow consists of five key operations implemented through specialized helper functions. Virtual Call Detection. CollectCalls extracts all call instructions from the function (Line 1 ), then IsVirtualCall identifies virtual calls by analyzing instruction patterns (Line 5 ). Virtual calls exhibit the characteristic pattern of indirect calls through vtable pointers with fixed offsets, distinguishing them from other indirect calls such as function pointer invocations. Object Provenance Tracing. For each confirmed virtual call, TraceObject identifies the object used at the call site by analyzing register usage and stack variables (Line 6 ). FollowObjectChain then traces this object backward through its definition chain, analyzing cross-references to identify constructor invocations and vtable assignments (Line 9 ). This backward analysis collects candidate vtables that the object might reference at runtime, handling polymorphic scenarios where multiple derived classes are possible. Method Resolution. ParseMethodOffset extracts the method offset from the call instruction by analyzing the vtable dereference pattern (Line 10 ). LookupVtable then reads the vtable structure at each candidate address, retrieving the function pointer at the computed offset (Lines 12 – 15 ). When multiple candidate vtables exist, all possible targets are collected, providing agents with complete information about potential polymorphic dispatch targets. Integration with Decompilation. The resolved mappings ℛ \mathcal{R} integrate into the decompilation pipeline, where resolved calls appear as comments in the pseudocode output formatted as // Resolved virtual call: function_name at 0xaddress . This annotation enables agents to understand complete call chains without manual vtable analysis. We analyze tool invocation patterns across all 12 agent-model configurations from the Stage 2 PoC generation evaluation ( § 6.4 ). Across 480 case-executions (12 configurations × \times 40 cases), agents invoke 39,334 MCP tool calls (81.9 per case on average) spanning tools from all three MCP servers in Table 1 . We group tools into Binary Exploration (decompilation, disassembly, cross-references), COM Inspection (interface discovery, proxy definitions, skeleton generation), and Dynamic Debugging (PoC submission, debugger control, process management). Table 9. Tool call distribution across three MCP server categories for all 12 PoC generation configurations. Calls : total tool invocations in the category. % : share of all tool calls for that configuration. The remaining share (not shown) corresponds to agent-native calls (file I/O, task management). Category distribution. As shown in Table 9 , Dynamic Debugging dominates across all configurations, accounting for 53.2% of all MCP tool calls (20,921 calls). This reflects the iterative compile-execute-debug cycle central to PoC generation, where agents repeatedly submit PoC variants via submit_poc (3,466 calls total, 7.2 per case), attach debuggers, inspect crash states, and resume execution. Binary Exploration accounts for 29.7% (11,678 calls), driven by search_functions (5,279 calls) and decompile_function_addr/name (3,885 calls) as agents analyze target functions and trace vulnerable code paths. COM Inspection accounts for 17.1% (6,735 calls), with get_proxy_definition (1,717 calls) and find_interface (1,527 calls) as the most frequent tools for resolving COM method signatures. Agent comparison. Slyp invokes more Binary Exploration tools than baselines (33.9% vs. 25.4% for Codex and 23.5% for Cc ), reflecting its structured approach of analyzing vulnerable function internals before writing PoC code. On a per-case basis, Slyp calls decompile_function_addr/name 10.1 times per case versus 6.1 for baselines, and search_functions 13.6 times versus 8.4. Cc allocates a larger share to Dynamic Debugging (61.6%) with proportionally fewer Binary Exploration calls, indicating a trial-and-error strategy that submits PoC variants earlier and relies on crash feedback to iteratively converge on the correct trigger. Role of skeleton code. The generate_skeleton_code tool produces compilable C++ templates that instantiate a target COM object and invoke its interface methods with correct GUIDs, threading model, and activation boilerplate. All 12 configurations invoke this tool, totaling 744 calls (1.6 per case on average), confirming that every agent relies on it as a foundational step. Without this tool, the agent must manually construct COM activation code, a process that requires resolving CLSIDs, interface IIDs, apartment threading setup ( CoInitializeEx ), and server-specific security attributes. Prior work on COM vulnerability exploitation assumes manually authored harness code ( gu2022comrace ) , and no existing binary analysis framework provides automated COM harness generation. The generate_skeleton_code tool eliminates this manual effort and enables agents to focus on the vulnerability-specific race condition trigger logic rather than COM plumbing, a prerequisite for fully automated PoC generation at scale.

Table 10 lists the knowledge cutoff dates, public release dates, and context window sizes of all evaluated models. The knowledge cutoff date indicates the latest date through which a model’s pre-training data extends. The latest knowledge cutoff across all models is August 2025 (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-mini, GPT-5.3-codex, and Sonnet 4.6). Table 10. Twelve of the 20 COM objects in the evaluation dataset contain zero-day vulnerabilities initially discovered during Slyp ’s research campaign ( § 6.2 ). A potential concern is that if the ground truth were derived from Slyp ’s own output, any vulnerabilities Slyp fails to detect would be excluded by construction, artificially inflating recall. The dataset includes 8 COM objects containing independently discovered 1-day vulnerabilities that Slyp never encountered during development ( § 6.1.2 ). On this subset, Slyp achieves an average F1 of 0.943 across all six models, compared to 0.912 on the full 20-case dataset ( § 6.3 ). The comparable or higher performance on cases Slyp never analyzed confirms that circularity does not inflate the reported metrics.
