Simplified code snippet for CVE-2025-53802. We present a detailed analysis of CVE-2025-53802 ( 21 ), a use-after-free vulnerability discovered by Slyp . The vulnerability arises from the interaction between put_ScanResponsePayload and get_ScanResponsePayload that operate on the same COM reference pointer at offset 192, shown in Figure 5 . put_ScanResponsePayload updates this pointer via operator= , which sequentially increments the new object’s reference count, reads the current pointer, stores the new pointer, and decrements the old object’s reference count, all without synchronization. Concurrently, get_ScanResponsePayload reads the same pointer and calls InternalAddRef using a virtual AddRef_Proxy . When two setter threads run while a third executes the getter, both setters decrement the same old object’s reference count to zero and free it. The getter then dereferences a freed pointer during InternalAddRef , causing a use-after-free that enables local privilege escalation from Low to System integrity. Slyp performance. Slyp successfully identifies this vulnerability with precise localization through LLM-based semantic reasoning. Although Slyp cannot recover the complete virtual function table due to cross-module dependencies, the LLM agent infers the functionality of InternalAddRef and InternalRelease based on their names and calling context. By recognizing these as standard COM reference counting operations, Slyp correctly determines that they manipulate shared object state without synchronization and pinpoints the vulnerable access within InternalAddRef where the freed object is dereferenced. The agent then synthesizes the precise interleaving scenario (two put_ScanResponsePayload calls followed by one get_ScanResponsePayload call) that triggers the race window, providing detailed thread interaction patterns that enable efficient verification. Comparison with COMRace . COMRace fails to recover two main methods due to incomplete vtable resolution, resulting in zero detection. COMRace++ , our improved reproduction with extended vtable recovery ( Appendix B ), successfully recovers both methods and flags them as vulnerable to race conditions, achieving perfect recall on this case. However, COMRace++ fails to resolve the virtual calls within InternalRelease and InternalAddRef , which breaks propagation to reference-count functions and contributes to missed localization of the true unsafe site. As a result, it reports line 7 of get_ScanResponsePayload (a benign copy operation for this+192 ) as the vulnerable point, missing the actual vulnerability within InternalAddRef where freed objects are accessed. Given the granularity at which VEX IR represents memory effects, precisely trimming benign expressions/statements without suppressing security-relevant accesses is challenging, as discussed in § 6.5 . Mitigating this requires extensive engineering to tune filters and heuristics that separate benign patterns from genuine vulnerabilities, underscoring the specialization burden. In contrast, Slyp ’s semantic reasoning distinguishes the benign field copy from the critical freed-object access, demonstrating superior precision in vulnerability localization.
sections.raw_text: spacing=nonfrench Agentic Vulnerability Reasoning on Windows COM Binaries Hwiwon Lee ∗ Jongseong Kim ∗ Lingming Zhang University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign Abstract. Windows Component Object Model (COM) services run with elevated privileges and are widely accessible to authenticated users, making race conditions in these binaries a critical surface for local privilege escalation. We present Slyp , an end-to-end agentic pipeline that discovers race condition vulnerabilities in COM binaries and generates debugger-verified proof-of-concept (PoC) code. Slyp exposes binary exploration, COM inspection, and dynamic debugging as reusable tool interfaces, giving agents the static context, COM activation metadata, and debugger feedback needed to move from vulnerability discovery to verified PoC generation. On a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases, Slyp achieves 0.973 F1, outperforming production coding agents by up to 0.208 F1 and the state-of-the-art static analyzer by 3.3 × \times in bug discovery. For PoC generation, production coding agents in their default setup (without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools) verify essentially no cases on either frontier model, whereas Slyp ’s interactive toolsets enable it to autonomously synthesize working PoCs for 67.5% of cases on the strongest configuration. Deployed on production Windows services, Slyp discovers 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across nine COM services, all confirmed by the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) with 16 CVEs assigned and $140,000 in bounties. Furthermore, Slyp is designed with generalizable binary analysis and debugging interfaces, making it readily applicable to other commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries beyond Windows COM services. 1 1 footnotetext: Equal contribution. 1. Introduction Windows Component Object Model (COM) services constitute a vast attack surface for local privilege escalation. Hundreds of privileged COM classes run in Windows service processes with elevated privileges, and many are accessible to authenticated users through Remote Procedure Call (RPC) ( microsoft2024com ; gu2022comrace ) . Race conditions in these services arise when concurrent RPC threads access shared object state without synchronization, producing use-after-free and double-free vulnerabilities that yield arbitrary code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service ( gu2022comrace ; upadhyay2023navigating ) . These vulnerabilities are prevalent across Windows installations yet remain difficult to detect. Analysis requires reasoning about thread interleavings, object lifetimes, and locking protocols simultaneously across functions that optimizing compilers strip of all type and symbol information. Existing approaches to detecting race conditions in COM binaries face fundamental limitations. Coverage-guided fuzzers lack control over thread scheduling to trigger specific interleavings ( jeong2019razzer ) . Static analyzers such as COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) rely on exhaustive pair enumeration that produces false positives from incorrect alias results and unmodeled input conditions, achieving only 0.299 F1 in our evaluation ( § 6.5 ). Structural assumptions about register usage and vtable layouts further miss vulnerabilities across diverse binary patterns. Manual reverse engineering requires decompiling each entry function, tracing shared-state accesses, and checking locking discipline across many functions, which does not scale to production COM services. In recent years, Large language models (LLMs) offer a complementary capability. LLMs demonstrate strong semantic reasoning over decompiled code, recovering variable names, data structures, and types from binaries ( xie2024resym ; xu2025gennm ) . Agentic systems extend this by scaffolding LLM reasoning with external tool invocation and iterative validation ( yao2022react ; yang2024swe ; wang2024openhands ) . Pioneering work demonstrates that LLM agents can discover real-world vulnerabilities that elude traditional fuzzers ( projectzero2024naptime ; projectzero2024bigsleep ) . The Model Context Protocol (MCP) ( anthropic2024mcp ) standardizes tool interfaces, enabling agents such as Codex ( codex2025 ) and Claude Code ( Cc ) ( claudecode2026 ) to invoke binary analysis tools autonomously. The missing piece is a structured pipeline that channels LLM reasoning toward vulnerability-specific analysis patterns and extends from discovery through automated proof-of-concept (PoC) generation. We present Slyp , the first end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and generating debugger-verified PoC programs. Slyp provides three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration, COM inspection, and debugging. Together they cover decompilation and virtual dispatch resolution, interface metadata and activation templates, as well as live PoC compilation, execution, and crash capture. The agent follows a standard ReAct loop ( yao2022react ) with engineering features purpose-built for long-horizon binary analysis. These include pre-compaction checkpointing that preserves cross-function findings across context compression and task verification that prevents premature termination. The pipeline is model-agnostic and works across GPT and Claude models without modification. We evaluate Slyp on discovery and PoC generation against production coding agents ( Codex and Cc ) across six backbone models with identical task prompts. On a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases, Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 (GPT-5.4), while production agents with default minimal binary analysis tools reach 0.910 ( Codex , GPT-5.4) and 0.724 ( Cc , Opus 4.6). Augmenting production agents with all binary exploration tools narrows but does not close the gap, with Slyp still leading on every tested model by up to +0.208 F1. Moreover, Slyp also outperforms COMRace++ (our enhanced reproduction of the state-of-the-art static analyzer COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) ) by 3.3 × \times (0.973 vs. 0.299). On PoC generation, production agents without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools solve essentially no cases, while the best Slyp configuration solves 67.5% (27/40) and the four top configurations collectively solve 34 of 40. Deployed on production Windows services, Slyp has discovered 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across 9 COM services. MSRC has confirmed all 28 vulnerabilities with 16 CVEs and awarded $140,000 in bounties. This paper makes the following contributions: • We build three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration ( § 4.3 ), COM inspection ( § 4.4 ), and dynamic debugging ( § 4.5 ) that integrate with any MCP-compatible agent and are the primary contributors to both vulnerability discovery and PoC generation. • We design and implement Slyp , an end-to-end agentic pipeline that combines a ReAct loop with engineering features for long-horizon binary analysis, outperforming production coding agents on every tested model for vulnerability discovery ( § 6.3 ) and PoC generation ( § 6.4 ). While this paper primarily focuses on Windows COM binaries, Slyp can be easily generalized to other commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries. • We construct a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases with manually-verified ground-truth labels, including 8 independently discovered 1-day CVEs for circularity-free validation, and provide agent trajectory artifacts across three scaffolds and six backbone models ( § 6 ). • We demonstrate practical impact through the discovery of 28 zero-day vulnerabilities across 9 production Windows services, all confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs assigned and bounties totaling $140,000 ( § 6.2 ). 2. Background COM as a Privilege Escalation Surface. Windows Component Object Model (COM) is a binary interface standard for inter-process software composition ( microsoft2024com ) . Hundreds of COM classes are hosted in Windows service processes that run with elevated privileges. A low-privileged client activates a COM object by calling an activation function such as CoCreateInstance with the target class identifier (CLSID). The COM runtime routes the request across a process boundary via RPC over Advanced Local Procedure Call (ALPC) into the privileged service. Two permission layers gate access. Launch permission controls whether the client can start the server process, and access permission controls whether the client can invoke methods on a running object ( gu2022comrace ) . Many system services grant both permissions to all authenticated users, and some grant access to AppContainer-sandboxed processes with specific capabilities. A memory corruption vulnerability in any reachable interface method gives the attacker code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service, making cross-process COM a dense, high-value attack surface for local privilege escalation. Interfaces, Vtables, and the Binary Reality. Each COM class implements one or more typed interfaces, all ultimately deriving from IUnknown ( QueryInterface , AddRef , Release ). Interface methods are dispatched through virtual function tables (vtables) stored in the binary, where each vtable entry holds a pointer to a concrete function ( gu2022comrace ; zhang2023comfusion ) . Cross-process calls use a proxy/stub pair that marshals arguments via RPC between client and server ( microsoft2024com ) . The Windows registry maps each CLSID to its binary path, threading model, AppID, service name, and hosting account. In production binaries, interface methods appear as indirect call sequences through vtable pointers at fixed offsets, obscuring the interface hierarchy and concrete targets ( shoshitaishvili2016sok ; pawlowski2017marx ) . Recovering virtual call targets is a prerequisite for tracing data flows ( § 4.3 ). Threading Model and Root Cause of Races. COM uses apartments to govern concurrency ( gu2022comrace ) . A single-threaded apartment (STA) serializes all incoming calls through a Windows message queue, making objects thread-safe by construction. A multi-threaded apartment (MTA) allows concurrent method calls from multiple RPC threads, requiring objects to synchronize shared state themselves. Most cross-process system services register as MTA for performance, so concurrent client requests execute on separate RPC threads with no framework-provided locking. If an interface method reads or writes a member field ( this+offset ) without holding a lock, the field is a data race candidate. Conflicting accesses on the same field (read/free, write/free, free/free, read/write) produce use-after-free, double-free, and type confusion vulnerabilities ( gu2022comrace ; upadhyay2023navigating ) . 1 __int64 CWorkflowSession::SetPrintTicket ( 2 CWorkflowSession * this , 3 int a2, const unsigned __int8 * a3) 4 { 5 void * v7; 6 ... 7 // [1] Read Shared Field 8 v4 = ( void * ) * ((_QWORD * ) this + 10 ); 9 if ( v4 ) 10 // [2] Free Heap 11 operator delete [](v4); 12 v7 = operator new []( * (( unsigned int * ) this + 22 ), 13 ( const struct std : : nothrow_t * ) & std : : nothrow); 14 // [3] Store New Pointer to Shared Field 15 * ((_QWORD * ) this + 10 ) = v7; 16 if ( v7 ) 17 // [4] Write Data to Heap 18 memcpy_0(v7, a3, * (( unsigned int * ) this + 22 )); 19 ... 20 return v5; 21 } Figure 1. CVE-2024-49095: Race condition in SetPrintTicket of PrintWorkflowUserSvc. Figure 2. Two thread interleavings of concurrent SetPrintTicket calls on the same object, producing a use-after-free (left) and a double-free (right). Running Example. Figure 1 shows the SetPrintTicket method of the CWorkflowSession COM class, hosted in PrintWorkflowUserSvc, a per-user service that manages the Windows Print Workflow. The method first reads the heap pointer stored at the shared field *((_QWORD *)this + 10) into a local variable (line 8) and, if non-null, frees the old buffer via operator delete[] (line 11). It then allocates a fresh buffer with operator new[] (line 12), stores the returned pointer back into *((_QWORD *)this + 10) (line 15), and copies print-ticket data into the buffer via memcpy (line 18). Because CWorkflowSession is registered under the MTA threading model, the COM runtime dispatches concurrent RPC requests to SetPrintTicket on the same object from separate threads with no framework-provided serialization. The entire read–free–allocate–write sequence on *((_QWORD *)this + 10) therefore executes without mutual exclusion, and two distinct vulnerability classes arise depending on the thread interleaving. Figure 2 illustrates both dangerous interleavings. In the use-after-free scenario ( Figure 2 (a)), Thread 1 allocates a new buffer (line 12) and stores the returned pointer into the shared field *((_QWORD *)this + 10) (line 15), exposing the new allocation to other threads. Before Thread 1 reaches the memcpy (line 18), Thread 2 enters the method, reads the pointer that Thread 1 just stored (line 8), and frees it (line 11). Thread 1 then proceeds to write data into the now-freed buffer, producing a use-after-free. In the double-free scenario ( Figure 2 (b)), both threads check the allocation at *((_QWORD *)this + 10) and find it non-null (line 9) before either performs the free. Each thread independently invokes operator delete[] on that pointer (line 11), causing a double-free. Both vulnerabilities are security-relevant because a low-privileged client can trigger concurrent RPC invocations on the shared object from separate threads. This unsynchronized read–free–write pattern on shared fields recurs across Windows system services and constitutes the primary vulnerability class Slyp targets. Detecting these patterns requires understanding COM threading semantics, tracing shared-state access across decompiled functions, and verifying that no synchronization primitive protects each access path. 3. Motivation and Problem Statement Why an Agentic System for COM Binaries. We adopt an agentic architecture where an LLM plans, invokes tools, and iterates with verification, rather than relying on monolithic static analyses or unguided prompting. This design choice rests on four empirical observations. First, LLMs exhibit strong semantic reasoning over binaries, recovering variable names, types, and data structures from executables without requiring bespoke analyzers ( xie2024resym ; xu2025gennm ) . Second, agentic orchestration generalizes across targets through prompt and tool adjustments, avoiding the multi-year engineering effort required to extend traditional analyzers to new platforms ( luo2026agentic ; yang2024swe ; wang2024openhands ) . Third, iterative think-act loops grounded in concrete tool outputs mitigate hallucination and sustain progress over long-horizon analyses, as demonstrated by systems coupling LLM planning with execution and constraint solving ( yao2022react ; hu2024treeplanner ) . Fourth, agentic workflows enable validation through decompilation-based data flow analysis, targeted execution, and call-graph tracing, corroborating bug hypotheses with evidence before reporting and improving precision over pattern-only detectors ( luo2026agentic ) . Target: Windows COM Services. We target Windows COM services ( § 2 ), which constitute ideal subjects for evaluating scalable vulnerability discovery in closed-source binaries. COM services present four properties that make them compelling targets: ❶ they are ubiquitous across Windows installations, ❷ they frequently run with elevated system privileges, ❸ they expose complex attack surfaces through multiple interfaces with well-defined entry points, and ❹ they exhibit significant detection challenges due to intricate reference counting, locking protocols, and virtual dispatch semantics that optimizing compilers obscure or eliminate in resulting binaries. As discussed in § 2 , memory corruption in any reachable interface method of a cross-process COM service yields code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service. COM interfaces provide well-defined entry points for systematic analysis while presenting the full complexity of production binaries. Problem Scope: Race Condition Vulnerabilities. Given a target COM binary and entry function addresses, the task is to determine whether race condition vulnerabilities are reachable through these entry points, localize the root cause, and generate a proof-of-concept program that triggers the vulnerability. While Slyp targets race conditions as its primary vulnerability class, the architecture supports other classes by modifying task prompts and tool configurations. Race conditions occur when concurrent threads access or modify shared objects without appropriate synchronization, leading to use-after-free or double-free bugs that manifest only under specific thread interleavings ( gu2022comrace ; jeong2019razzer ; upadhyay2023navigating ) . We focus on memory corruption arising from unsynchronized accesses to shared objects across concurrent COM interface invocations, including setter/getter interactions, cross-thread object lifetimes, and improper reference counting. Successful analysis demands tracking object lifetimes and argument flows across function boundaries, reasoning about locking protocols and synchronization primitives, and synthesizing evidence across multiple execution paths to confirm vulnerabilities. Limitations of Existing Approaches. Static analysis tools for binary vulnerability detection each target a narrow bug class with bespoke engines: COMRace models COM locking protocols ( gu2022comrace ) , COMFusion recovers types to find union type confusions ( zhang2023comfusion ) , and QueryX enables symbolic queries over decompiled binaries ( han2023queryx ) . Each requires predefined vulnerability signatures, substantial per-target engineering, and handcrafted queries from domain experts, limiting adaptability to unfamiliar locking idioms or new platforms ( upadhyay2023navigating ; jeong2019razzer ) . Fuzzers, including traditional pipelines ( oss-fuzz ; jeong2019razzer ) and recent LLM-driven approaches ( deng2023large ; xia2024fuzz4all ) , achieve broad code coverage but struggle with the narrow timing windows of race conditions, while general-purpose binary analysis frameworks lack COM-specific threading and activation models ( schilling2024binarytsan ) . Why End-to-End Matters. Discovery alone yields candidate vulnerabilities that can include hallucinations, forcing domain experts to separate true positives from false reports. An end-to-end pipeline that continues through PoC generation and debugger verification filters discovery outputs that fail to produce crashes and reduces manual effort by generating actionable crash evidence such as stack traces, register dumps, and faulting call stacks. COMRace generates template-based PoCs but cannot iteratively debug them against a live service ( gu2022comrace ) . Slyp closes this gap through an iterative compile-execute-debug loop that refines PoC programs until a debugger-attached crash is captured ( § 4.5 ). The resulting crash artifacts directly support triage and patch prioritization, converting agent output into actionable security reports. Since the MCP tools are target-agnostic and domain knowledge resides in prompts, Slyp generalizes to other binary targets with minimal engineering effort such as prompt revisions and tool interface adjustments. 4. Design 4.1. Overview Figure 3. Overview of Slyp . A single ReAct agent operates in two stages connected by a structured vulnerability report. In Stage 1 (Discovery), the agent uses only the IDA MCP server for binary exploration. In Stage 2 (PoC Generation), the agent uses all three MCP servers: IDA for evidence re-checking, COM inspection for activation metadata and skeleton generation, and dynamic debugging for iterative compile-execute-debug refinement until a debugger-verified PoC package is produced. Figure 3 illustrates the architecture of Slyp , an end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in COM binaries and generating debugger-verified proof-of-concept (PoC) programs. The pipeline operates in two stages connected by a structured vulnerability report. In Stage 1 (Discovery), an LLM agent uses binary exploration tools to navigate decompiled code, resolve virtual dispatch through COM vtables, trace shared-state data flows, and identify race condition vulnerabilities across target entry functions. In Stage 2 (PoC Generation), the agent uses COM inspection tools to retrieve interface metadata and activation templates, then uses debugging tools to compile, execute, and debug C++ PoC programs against a live COM service, iteratively refining until a debugger-attached crash is captured. Slyp exposes three categories of MCP tool interfaces. Binary exploration ( § 4.3 ) provides decompilation with integrated virtual dispatch resolution, disassembly, cross-references, and call graph navigation for static binary analysis. COM inspection ( § 4.4 ) exposes COM registry enumeration, interface declarations, security descriptors, and skeleton code generation for understanding COM activation requirements. Dynamic debugging ( § 4.5 ) supports PoC compilation, execution, crash capture, and heap diagnostics against a live Windows environment. All tools are accessible through the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ( anthropic2024mcp ) , enabling any MCP-compatible agent to invoke the same interfaces. The pipeline is model-agnostic. Algorithm 1 ReAct Agent Loop 1: Input: Task instruction I I , target functions 𝒜 \mathcal{A} , Environment E E 2: Output: Result r ∈ { finished , interrupted } r\in\{\mbox{{finished}},\mbox{{interrupted}}\} 3: Notation: 𝒯 \mathcal{T} : trajectory (sequence of think-act steps); 4: ℳ \mathcal{M} : reasoning model; ω a \omega_{a} : action; ω o \omega_{o} : observation 5: Parameters: n max n_{\max} : step limit 6: 1: 𝒯 ← { I , 𝒜 } \mathcal{T}\leftarrow\{I,\mathcal{A}\} 2: 3: for n max n_{\max} steps do 4: ω a ← ℳ ​ ( Serialize ​ ( 𝒯 ) ) \omega_{a}\leftarrow\mathcal{M}(\textsc{Serialize}(\mathcal{T})) ⊳ \triangleright Think and select action 5: if IsDone ​ ( ω a ) \textsc{IsDone}(\omega_{a}) then 6: return ( finished , E ) (\mbox{{finished}},E) 7: E , ω o ← Execute ​ ( E , ω a ) E,\omega_{o}\leftarrow\textsc{Execute}(E,\omega_{a}) ⊳ \triangleright Dispatch to tools 8: 𝒯 ← 𝒯 ∪ { ⟨ ω a , ω o ⟩ } \mathcal{T}\leftarrow\mathcal{T}\cup\{\langle\omega_{a},\omega_{o}\rangle\} ⊳ \triangleright Append to trajectory 9: 10: return ( interrupted , E ) (\mbox{{interrupted}},E) 4.2. Agent Architecture Slyp implements a standard ReAct agent ( yao2022react ) , the same think-act-observe paradigm used by production coding agents such as Codex ( codex2025 ) and Cc ( claudecode2026 ) . Algorithm 1 formalizes the shared loop: at each step, the reasoning model ℳ \mathcal{M} generates an action ω a \omega_{a} (a tool call or final response), the tool interface dispatches the call to the appropriate MCP server or the built-in tool and returns an observation ω o \omega_{o} , and the model reasons about the result before selecting the next action. The loop terminates when the agent produces a structured vulnerability report or exhausts the step budget. A middleware pipeline runs before each turn and before the agent stops, enabling the context management and verification behaviors described below. The differences between agents lie in specific engineering choices within this shared framework, not in a fundamentally different architecture. Context Management. Long-horizon binary analysis generates large trajectories. A single decompilation produces 2,000+ tokens, and examining dozens of entry functions with their callees generates 100K+ tokens. Slyp addresses context growth through auto-compaction with structured checkpointing. A middleware monitor tracks the running token count and triggers compaction when usage approaches the context window limit. The compaction process proceeds in four phases. First, the system preserves recent messages by walking backward from the most recent turn, keeping tool-call/response pairs intact. Second, it extracts all substantive assistant messages and saves them to an external checkpoint file, creating a lossless side-channel that bypasses the lossy summary. Third, a compaction model generates a structured summary following a seven-section template (task, methodology, per-item findings with evidence, cross-item analysis, coverage, external state, current work state) that preserves cross-function relationships and analytical state. Fourth, a post-compaction directive instructs the agent to read the saved checkpoint, re-verify tentative conclusions, and continue analysis. If a single tool output exceeds the context window before compaction triggers, the system applies progressive truncation at three levels until the context fits. Memory. Slyp provides a file-based memory system organized as a compact index plus topic files. The index file is loaded into the agent’s context at each turn and serves as a quick reference with section headers, inline findings, and links to detailed topic files. Each topic file stores one durable analytical finding and begins with YAML frontmatter containing a name, description, and type. During long analyses, the agent incrementally saves function summaries, vulnerability patterns, and cross-function relationships into these topic files so they survive compaction. The index remains size-bounded so it stays cheap to reload, while the detailed evidence stays in topic files until the agent needs it. Retrieval uses BM25 search ( robertson2009bm25 ) augmented with frontmatter text. For each topic file, the searcher prepends the name and description fields before tokenization, which boosts files whose declared purpose matches the query without extra model calls or hand-tuned field weights. Because the search is deterministic and local, the agent can recover specific evidence after compaction with low latency. This indexed design lets Slyp revisit prior findings without re-reading the full interaction history. In this way, investigation depth can grow on disk while the active context stays bounded. Task Verification. We use verifier-style middleware to keep the agent aligned with the task throughout long analysis sessions. Instead of trusting the agent’s self-assessment, the middleware inspects observable execution state and injects corrective feedback when the trajectory shows that the task has not yet been carried out or no usable answer has been produced. This improves reliability by grounding control decisions in concrete execution properties rather than the model’s stated intent. Furthermore, it can prevent the premature termination on weaker models. A stop-time verifier intervenes when the agent attempts to terminate without demonstrating meaningful progress toward the task. In task-oriented sessions, it prevents plan-only exits by requiring actual tool use and it also catches empty final turns after tool execution, forcing the agent to continue until it emits a usable artifact. During long trajectories, a token-budget-driven memory nudge asks the agent to consolidate durable findings into external memory and re-check that the current line of work still matches the original objective. As this nudge is triggered by context growth rather than fixed turn counts, it preserves analytical state during tool-heavy phases without disrupting shorter reasoning phases. 4.3. Binary Exploration Analyzing compiled binaries requires specialized tools that differ fundamentally from source code analysis. Even with debug symbols, binaries lack the full semantic context available in source code: object layouts, type hierarchies, and inter-procedural data flows must be recovered through decompilation and cross-reference analysis. COM binaries present an additional challenge through virtual dispatch via vtable pointers, making indirect calls opaque to standard decompilation ( shoshitaishvili2016sok ; pang2021sok ) . We implement a suite of binary exploration tools through MCP ( anthropic2024mcp ) , providing a unified interface for vulnerability analysis over compiled code. Table 1 lists the agent-facing tools exposed during evaluation. Code reconstruction tools ( decompile_function_addr/name , disassemble_function ) transform binary code into high-level pseudocode with integrated virtual function call resolution or instruction-level assembly. get_function_by_name and get_function_by_address retrieve function metadata including bounds and argument arity. Navigation tools enable tracing how data and control flow propagate through call chains. get_xrefs_to identifies all code locations referencing a given address, enabling agents to locate all uses of shared state or calls to critical functions. get_callees and get_callers enumerate direct call relationships, and search_functions finds matched functions by name patterns. Virtual function calls in COM binaries appear as indirect calls through vtable pointers, obscuring the actual target function ( pawlowski2017marx ; schwartz2018ooanalyzer ) . We integrate virtual function call resolution directly into the decompilation process, automatically resolving virtual calls and annotating them in the pseudocode output. For each indirect call, the system identifies whether it represents a virtual call by analyzing the instruction pattern. An object tracer then follows the object backward through its definition chain, collecting candidate vtables by analyzing cross-references and constructor invocations. The system parses the method offset, looks up the concrete function in each candidate vtable, and annotates the pseudocode with resolved function names and addresses. This eliminates the need for agents to arduously reason about COM virtual dispatch and ensures that resolved targets appear as concrete callees in navigation results. Vtables loaded dynamically from external binaries and polymorphic initialization from multiple classes remain unresolved by this static technique. The detailed recovery procedure is provided in Appendix C (Algorithm 2 ). Table 1. MCP tools provided to the agent across three servers: binary exploration (9 tools), COM inspection (14 tools), and dynamic debugging (10 tools). 4.4. COM Inspection Writing a PoC for a COM race condition requires knowing how to activate the target COM object such as which CLSID to use, which interfaces to query, what method signatures look like, and what privilege level the service runs at. This information resides in the Windows registry and in proxy/stub DLLs, not in the target binary itself. We expose COM registry inspection as a dynamic MCP interface, giving the LLM agent on-demand access to the live COM metadata of the target Windows system. The tool interface provides 14 tools organized by workflow stage ( Table 1 ). Service discovery locates target COM services and their hosting model. Class and interface enumeration retrieves classes, interfaces, and metadata (threading model, privilege level, AppContainer accessibility) with separate paths for WinRT and classic COM CLSIDs. Security analysis retrieves launch and access permissions in SDDL format to determine whether an unprivileged client can activate the target. Interface inspection retrieves full method signatures in Interface Definition Language (IDL) or C++ format from proxy/stub DLLs. Code generation produces compilable C++ skeletons with the correct activation pattern ( CoCreateInstance for classic COM, RoActivateInstance or RoGetActivationFactory for WinRT) working as a placeholder for crafting a PoC. These tools enable hierarchical COM registry traversal. The agent locates the target service, enumerates its classes and interfaces, retrieves method signatures via proxy definitions, and generates a skeleton PoC. This skeleton becomes the starting point for the iterative PoC refinement loop in Stage 2. 4.5. Dynamic Debugging As discussed in § 3 , Stage 1 still produces candidate vulnerabilities that require crash evidence to reject hallucinated reports and confirm true positives. Race condition PoCs are especially challenging because they require correct COM activation, a workable thread interleaving, and iterative debugging against a live service. Stage 2 addresses this with automated PoC generation paired with debugger-based crash verification. The PoC pipeline receives Stage 1’s vulnerability report as input. The agent extracts the vulnerable COM class, affected entry functions, race condition type, and suggested interleaving from the structured report. It then uses COM inspection tools ( § 4.4 ) to retrieve interface definitions and generate a skeleton C++ template, and writes the concurrent invocation logic to trigger the identified race window. The agent enters an iterative compile-execute-debug loop ( xia2024automated ) , submitting PoC source code, receiving compilation errors or execution results (crash dump, timeout, or clean exit), and refining the source based on the feedback. We enable page heap on the target VM so that race-induced heap corruptions are caught deterministically at the faulting instruction. A PoC is verified when the crash dump shows an access violation or heap corruption and the faulting call stack still reaches the target function from Stage 1. The tool interface provides 10 debugging tools in four categories. PoC submission compiles C++ source on the target VM and captures output and crash evidence in a single tool call. Command execution tools run arbitrary debugger commands and command sequences for targeted analysis. Process and service management tools enumerate running processes, start services, and attach or detach the debugger. VM control tools break into the debugger and resume VM execution when the target service requires recovery. The agent typically requires 3 to 10 compile-debug iterations per successful PoC, with common refinement patterns including COM activation errors, thread count and timing adjustments, service crash recovery, and heap diagnostics. Each verified case produces four artifacts (C++ source, crash dump, call stack, register state) constituting the evidence package submitted to MSRC. 5. Implementation We implement Slyp as a 19.9K-line Python agent framework plus 8.7K lines of Python code across three MCP servers: Binary Exploration Server. The binary exploration server implements diverse tools on top of IDA Pro ( idapro ) , and the evaluation exposes the 9 agent-facing tools listed in Table 1 . A Python FastMCP server communicates with an IDA Pro plugin via HTTP JSON-RPC. The plugin runs inside IDA’s Python interpreter and exposes analysis functions as JSON-RPC endpoints. The MCP server auto-generates tool wrappers from plugin decorators via abstract syntax tree (AST) manipulation, eliminating boilerplate for each new tool. Vtable resolution logic and callee/caller enumeration are implemented from scratch atop IDA Hex-Rays. Core decompilation uses IDA Hex-Rays together with official Microsoft debug symbols from the public symbol server. COM Inspection Server. The COM inspection server exposes 14 tools built on OleViewDotNet ( oleviewdotnet ) . A Python FastMCP server on the host communicates with a C# .NET 4.8.1 application inside the target Windows VM via a TCP JSON-line protocol. The .NET application queries the live Windows COM registry and WinRT APIs. This is the same underlying tool used by COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) for COM registry inspection, exposed as a dynamic MCP interface that the LLM agent queries on demand. Dynamic Debugging Server. The debugging server exposes the 10 agent-facing tools used in evaluation and relies on a larger backend built on WinDbg and Microsoft Visual C++ (MSVC). A Python FastMCP server communicates with a C++ WinDbg extension DLL via a Windows named pipe. The extension uses the WinDbg debugger engine API for kernel and user-mode debugging. PoC compilation and execution on the target VM are handled via Windows Remote Management (WinRM) and MSVC compiler. The exposed tool set covers PoC submission, debugger command execution, process and service management, and VM control. Agent and Infrastructure. The agent framework uses an async generator-based streaming architecture. A middleware pipeline with before-turn and before-stop hooks enables composable policies for compaction, memory nudging, and task verification ( § 4.2 ). MCP integration uses a shared registry that caches tool discovery across sessions and supports multiple transports. The same MCP servers work with Codex , Cc , and Slyp without modification. All evaluation runs execute against a QEMU-hosted Windows 11 VM with a userland debugger ( cdb.exe ) attached, using per-case snapshots from a base image for isolation. 6. Evaluation We evaluate Slyp to answer four research questions: • RQ1. How effective is Slyp at discovering zero-day vulnerabilities in production COM binaries? • RQ2. How does Slyp compare with state-of-the-art production agents in vulnerability discovery? • RQ3. How does Slyp compare with state-of-the-art production agents in PoC generation? • RQ4. How does Slyp compare to traditional static race condition analysis? 6.1. Experimental Setup 6.1.1. Dataset We curate a dataset of 20 Windows COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases from production systems across ten COM services. The dataset is constructed from two independent sources: ① 12 COM classes containing zero-day vulnerabilities across the 6 services from Table 2 , all independently confirmed by MSRC. ② 8 COM classes containing 1-day vulnerabilities across 7 services (3 overlapping with the zero-day set and 4 additional: McpManagementService , CDPSvc , camsvc , and InstallService ), discovered by independent researchers through standard MSRC channels and providing a circularity-free validation subset. We confine the vulnerability scope to use-after-free ( UAF ) and double-free ( DF ) conditions arising from race conditions, which lead to memory corruption and local privilege escalation. The 20 COM objects contain 379 entry functions in total, of which 64 are vulnerable, with per-object complexity ranging from 3 to 69 entry functions. We define vulnerable entry functions according to three criteria: ❶ if a race condition occurs in the entry function itself or its nested functions, we regard it as vulnerable. ❷ if the vulnerability results from interactions between setter and getter functions, we consider both as vulnerable. ❸ the vulnerable location must be reachable by a working proof-of-concept that crashes the service. A vulnerability case is a minimal, unique race condition pattern that demonstrates memory corruption in a COM service. A single case may involve one entry function (self-race) or a set of functions whose concurrent invocations complete the interleaving scenario ( e . g ., a setter/getter pair). The 20 COM objects yield 40 vulnerability cases spanning the 64 vulnerable entry functions. 6.1.2. Experimental Protocol We evaluate all agents on the full 20 COM objects under identical experimental conditions: ❶ binary exploration, COM inspection, and dynamic debugging tools exposed through standardized MCP interfaces, evaluated under complete and reduced toolset configurations as detailed in each RQ. ❷ identical task prompts describing the objective to discover race-condition-induced memory corruption vulnerabilities, with prompt templates in Appendix A . For discovery (RQ2, RQ4), we execute three independent runs per agent configuration and report best-of-3 per-case F1 by selecting the highest F1 across the three runs for each COM object, then macro-averaging across all 20 objects. Discovery requires the agent to analyze many entry functions per COM object without any validation mechanism, making both analysis coverage and output correctness sensitive to non-deterministic reasoning. For PoC generation (RQ3), we execute a single run per case because the task is inherently self-correcting: each case receives a scoped input (one or two vulnerable functions), and the dynamic debugging toolset provides interactive feedback through compilation, execution, and crash reporting, enabling the agent to iteratively correct errors. 6.1.3. Baselines We compare Slyp against two production-grade MCP-compatible coding agents: • Codex CLI ( Codex ) ( openai2025codex ) : OpenAI’s agentic coding system. We evaluate with GPT-5.4, GPT-5.3-codex, and GPT-5.4-mini. • Claude Code ( Cc ) : Anthropic’s agentic coding system. We evaluate with Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5. These are state-of-the-art production agents that (a) connect to MCP servers, (b) autonomously invoke tools in a ReAct-style loop ( yao2022react ) , and (c) manage 200K+ token context windows. All baselines connect to the same MCP servers as Slyp , with toolset configurations (complete or reduced) specified per experiment. There are no limits on budget or reasoning steps. Same-model pairing controls for model capability such as Slyp vs Codex on GPT-5.3-codex and GPT-5.4, Slyp vs Cc on Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.6. Within each model family, Slyp and the paired production agent share the same API endpoint, ensuring identical inference behavior across the comparison. In addition, we compare with COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) , a traditional static binary analysis tool for detecting race conditions in COM services. The original COMRace implementation is not publicly available and assumes vtable addresses are loaded exclusively via the rcx register, causing complete failure on diverse COM binaries. We reproduce COMRace and its extended version COMRace++ from scratch with improved vtable recovery logic, implementing COMRace++ in 1.6K lines of code (detailed in Appendix B ). 6.1.4. Data Leakage All zero-day patches in our dataset were released after the knowledge cutoff dates of all evaluated models ( Appendix E ), ensuring the 12 zero-day cases are definitively free from data leakage. For the 8 independently discovered 1-day cases, patches may predate the latest knowledge cutoff (August 2025). However, leakage is impractical because Microsoft distributes security fixes as cumulative binary updates without the binary-level details (field offsets, object layouts, synchronization patterns) that the analysis task requires, and Slyp operates on decompiled binary code from specific binary versions rather than source code or documentation. Performance on the 1-day subset confirms the absence of leakage, with Slyp achieving 0.943 average F1 across all six models ( § 6.3 ). 6.2. RQ1: Real-World Zero-Day Discovery We deploy Slyp on 6 Windows COM services to evaluate its effectiveness at discovering zero-day vulnerabilities in production binaries. We craft a domain-specific task prompt ( Appendix A ) encoding Windows synchronization patterns (critical sections, SRW locks, mutexes) and their APIs for detecting race conditions leading to UAF and DF in COM objects. We manually validate all findings and report them to MSRC. Table 2. Zero-day vulnerabilities by Slyp in six Windows COM services, all enabling local privilege escalation and reported to MSRC. “Merged”: shares a patch with another CVE but received a separate bounty. “Confirmed”: acknowledged by MSRC with a bounty award. As detailed in Table 2 , Slyp discovers 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across 9 COM services, all reported to MSRC with 16 assigned CVEs, 2 merged cases, and 10 confirmed with bounty awards. The discovered vulnerabilities include 24 use-after-free and 4 double-free bugs. All instances enable local privilege escalation (LPE): 22 escalate from Low to Medium integrity, 3 from Low to System integrity, and 3 from Medium to System integrity. All 28 vulnerabilities are discovered during Slyp ’s iterative research campaign across multiple model families. To our knowledge, Slyp is the first end-to-end agentic pipeline to autonomously discover zero-day vulnerabilities with working PoCs in production binaries. We discuss the circularity implications of including Slyp -discovered cases in the evaluation dataset in Appendix F . Summary: Slyp discovers 28 zero-day vulnerabilities enabling LPE across 9 COM services, all confirmed with 16 CVEs assigned and $140K in bounties. This is the first end-to-end demonstration of agentic vulnerability discovery in production binaries. 6.3. RQ2: Vulnerability Discovery We compare Slyp with state-of-the-art production coding agents ( Codex and Cc ) on the vulnerability discovery (Stage 1). Since production agents are not designed for bug discovery in Windows COM binaries, we augment them with our binary exploration tools and evaluate two variants per production agent. “Full” includes all nine binary exploration tools in Table 1 , while “Mini” uses only decompilation, disassembly, and symbol lookup ( get_function_by_name and get_function_by_address ). The baseline comparison runs on two frontier agent-model pairs, and the full-toolset evaluation extends to all 12 agent-model configurations to confirm Slyp ’s advantage generalizes across agents and models. Table 3. Discovery performance across toolset configurations on two frontier models. Slyp rows use the full pipeline with task verification. Full includes all 9 binary exploration tools. Calls/Tok: total MCP tool calls and total tokens in millions. Baseline Comparison. We compare Slyp against Codex (GPT-5.4) and Cc (Opus 4.6) under full and minimal tool configurations ( Table 3 ). First, Slyp substantially outperforms state-of-the-art coding agents under default minimal tool support. Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 with GPT-5.4 and 0.956 with Opus 4.6, while Codex with the minimal toolset reaches only 0.910 and Cc reaches 0.724, representing gaps of 0.063 and 0.232, respectively. Second, equipping production agents with the full toolset improves their performance significantly, though they remain below Slyp . Codex improves from 0.910 to 0.953 (+0.043) and Cc improves from 0.724 to 0.938 (+0.214), yet Slyp still leads by 0.020 and 0.018 due to its task verification mechanism that enforces exhaustive function coverage before producing a final report. Codex with GPT-5.4 partially compensates under the minimal tool configuration through longer reasoning chains that reconstruct call relationships from raw decompilation, while Cc with Opus 4.6 relies more heavily on the structured navigation the full toolset provides. Table 4. Per-case F1 on 20 COM objects with the full binary exploration toolset. Column headers show vulnerable / total entry functions per COM object. Ma F1 : macro-averaged F1. Mi F1 : micro-averaged F1. Filled boxes: zero-day cases (12); open boxes: independently discovered 1-day cases (8). Green cells: higher F1 within each same-model pair. Cross-Agent Generality. To confirm that the toolset benefit generalizes, we evaluate Slyp and existing code agents with our full toolset on more models, resulting in 12 agent-model configurations. As shown in Table 4 , all agents achieve strong discovery performance with the full toolset, with Slyp still outperforming the paired production agent on every model. Across all six models, Slyp achieves an average F1 of 0.912 compared to 0.863 for baselines with the full toolset, a consistent +0.049 improvement. Slyp achieves perfect recall (1.000) on the four strongest configurations (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.3-codex, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6), indicating complete vulnerability coverage. These results corroborate the baseline comparison, confirming that the shared toolset provides the analytical foundation and Slyp ’s task verification yields additional precision gains over existing production agents. Per-run F1 standard deviations across all 12 configurations range from 0.008 to 0.077, confirming Slyp ’s advantage is stable rather than an artifact of high-variance outliers. Same-model pairing isolates Slyp ’s pipeline contribution: Slyp (0.963) and Codex (0.960) match on GPT-5.3-codex, while gaps widen to +0.022 on Sonnet 4.6 and +0.208 on Haiku 4.5, showing task verification yields the largest gains when model capability is weaker, with practical implications for cost-sensitive deployment. On the 8 independently discovered 1-day COM objects, Slyp achieves 0.943 average F1 across all six models (vs. 0.889 for baselines), confirming evaluation circularity does not inflate Slyp ’s metrics. Summary: Slyp outperforms state-of-the-art production coding agents on every tested model, reaching 0.973 F1 with perfect recall in the best configuration. The binary exploration toolset drives discovery performance. Augmenting existing agents with our full toolset also improves their F1 by up to 0.214 on frontier models and enables consistent gains across all 12 configurations. 6.4. RQ3: Automated PoC Generation While RQ2 evaluates discovery at the granularity of 20 COM objects, RQ3 operates at the granularity of 40 individual vulnerability cases. Each case targets a single vulnerable function or a set of functions completing an interleaving scenario, and the agent receives a per-case vulnerability report from Stage 1. Only verified Stage 1 reports are passed to Stage 2, and all agents receive identical reports per case. Given this report, the agent writes, compiles, and executes C++ PoC code against a live COM service in a QEMU-hosted Windows 11 VM with cdb.exe attached via the WinDbg MCP server. The agent iteratively refines the PoC based on compilation errors and execution feedback until a debugger-attached crash is captured. The baseline comparison runs on two frontier agent-model pairs, and the full-toolset evaluation extends to all 12 agent-model configurations with a 1-hour wall-clock budget and 200 MCP tool calls per case. A PoC is verified when it triggers a crash in the target COM service process whose faulting instruction is an access violation, heap corruption, security check failure, or a break instruction raised by Windows AppVerifier ( microsoft2024appverifier ) , producing four artifacts per verified case (compilable C++ source code, process crash dump, faulting call stack, and register state at the crash point). Table 5. PoC generation performance comparing Slyp against Codex (GPT-5.4) and Cc (Opus 4.6) under progressive tool removal on the agents. BIN : binary exploration. COM : COM inspection. DBG : dynamic debugging. Tok : average tokens per case. Filled, half-filled, and open circles indicate full, partial, and no tool availability, respectively. Baseline Comparison. We compare Slyp against Codex (GPT-5.4) and Cc (Opus 4.6), then progressively augment the toolset on the production agents to isolate the contribution of Slyp ’s COM inspection and dynamic debugging as shown in Table 5 . Both reduced configurations replace the full binary exploration toolset with the minimal subset (decompile, disassemble, and symbol lookup) and remove all COM inspection tools; the first retains the submit_poc harness that compiles, executes, and reports crash results, while the second additionally removes that harness. Slyp with Opus 4.6 achieves the highest verified rate (27/40, 67.5%), while production agents in their default setup (without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools) solve essentially no cases. Even when augmented with the submit_poc harness that compiles, executes, and reports crash results, Codex and Cc solve only 13 cases each, 40.9% and 50.0% fewer than their fully-equipped counterparts (22 and 26 cases, respectively). Without COM activation metadata from OleViewDotNet, agents cannot construct the CLSID/IID initialization sequences that COM harnesses require, and without WinDbg feedback, agents cannot diagnose runtime failures or refine thread timing. Without the submission harness, both configurations solve 0 cases, confirming that execution feedback is the minimum requirement for PoC generation. Table 6. PoC generation results across 12 agent-model pairs on 40 cases with the full toolset. Rate : verified rate. Subs : total PoC submissions. BF : compilation failure rate. NC : compiles but no verified crash. Tok : average tokens per case. Green cells: higher rate within each same-model pair. Cross-Agent Results. We evaluate all 12 agent-model configurations with the full toolset. As shown in Table 6 , the best verified rate is 67.5%, achieved by Slyp with Opus 4.6 (27/40 cases), followed by Cc with Opus 4.6 at 65.0% (26/40). Slyp outperforms the paired production agent on all three Anthropic backbones, with the largest gain on Sonnet 4.6 (40.0% vs. 15.0%). Slyp also leads on two of three OpenAI models (57.5% vs. 55.0% on GPT-5.4 and 57.5% vs. 52.5% on GPT-5.3-codex), with only GPT-5.4-mini favoring Codex . The largest Slyp advantage (40.0% vs. 15.0%) occurs on Sonnet 4.6, where both configurations are severely timeout-constrained: 33 of 40 cases reach the 1-hour budget for Slyp and 26 of 40 for Cc . Despite this shared constraint, Slyp invokes 1.5 × \times more MCP tools (4,384 vs. 2,952) while generating 3.0 × \times fewer output tokens per case (114k vs. 343k), achieving 105.7 MCP calls per hour compared to 77.5 for Cc . Cc produces 4,641 output tokens per MCP call versus 1,049 for Slyp , indicating that extended reasoning between tool invocations consumes time budget without proportional progress. Slyp ’s staged pipeline mitigates this by providing focused context per phase, enabling 12 of 33 timed-out cases (36.4%) to reach verification compared to 3 of 26 (11.5%) for Cc . Slyp uses fewer tokens than production agents on five of six same-model pairs (up to 61.0% reduction on Haiku 4.5 vs. Cc , from 5.9M to 2.3M per case, and 22.8% on GPT-5.3-codex vs. Codex , from 8.3M to 6.4M), as the staged pipeline provides focused context at each phase rather than maintaining full conversation history. Figure 4. Overlap of verified cases among the four top configurations. We select the four-set combination that maximizes union coverage. The union covers 34 of 40 cases, and 16 cases are solved by all four. Complementarity. All 12 configurations collectively solve 34 of 40 cases (85.0%). Figure 4 plots the four-set combination that maximizes union coverage (34/40 cases with a 16-case intersection), showing that different agent-model pairs solve complementary subsets. The 6 unsolved cases involve multi-phase race conditions requiring both correct COM harness construction and precise thread timing that exceeds the 1-hour per-case budget. Failure Modes. Weaker configurations are bottlenecked by compilation (up to 46.7% per-submission build-failure rate on Sonnet 4.6 and 46.2% on Haiku 4.5), while frontier configurations shift the bottleneck to trigger reliability. The strongest pair ( Slyp with Opus 4.6) achieves 9.8% build failure and 44.3% no-crash rate, confirming that race window timing, not code generation, is the primary barrier for frontier models. Appendix D provides a detailed breakdown of tool invocation patterns across all 12 configurations. Summary: Slyp with Opus 4.6 achieves the highest PoC verification rate at 67.5% (27/40), outperforming production agents in their default setup which solve essentially no cases. COM inspection and dynamic debugging are the dominant enablers, with augmenting production agents from default to full toolset increasing verified cases by 69–100%. All 12 configurations collectively cover 34 of 40 cases (85.0%). 6.5. RQ4: Comparison with COMRace We compare Slyp against COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) , the state-of-the-art static race condition detector for COM objects. COMRace identifies cross-references to shared objects between methods without synchronization by tracking field access operations via taint propagation and flagging concurrent accesses without locking primitives. Reproduction and Enhancement. We develop COMRace++ with two core fixes and five engineering enhancements: all-register vtable store detection and this -pointer propagation (M1–M2) that enable the tool to function on real-world binaries, CFG-based traversal and per-field lock-set tracking (E1–E2), self-race detection and dereferenced pointer recursion (E3, E5), and R/R pair filtering (E4) that removes inherently benign read/read conflict pairs. We validate COMRace++ against the original COMRace datasets, confirming detection parity with reported capabilities. Full technical details appear in Appendix B . M1–M2 and E1–E3 are correctness-critical engineering fixes that allow the tool to produce usable output on real-world binaries and are therefore always enabled. We ablate only the heuristic precision enhancements E4 and E5, whose inclusion trades off false-positive reduction against true-positive loss. Table 7. Per-case precision (P), recall (R), and F1 of COMRace++ on 20 COM objects, ablating the heuristic precision enhancements E4 (R/R pair filtering) and E5 (pointer recursion) on top of the always-enabled correctness-critical core (M1–M2, E1–E3). Table 7 reports COMRace++ under three configurations. The best configuration (+E4/E5) achieves 0.299 F1, while Slyp with GPT-5.4 achieves 0.973 ( Table 4 ), a 3.3 × \times improvement. Even Slyp ’s weakest configuration (Haiku 4.5, 0.712) outperforms COMRace++ by 2.4 × \times . The gap is driven by low precision (0.145–0.158, 171–189 false positives from exhaustive pair enumeration) and incomplete recall (0.484–0.500, 32–33 missed vulnerable functions out of 64). Precision-Recall Analysis. COMRace++ ’s low precision stems from exhaustive pair enumeration: for each this+offset field accessed by n n methods with inconsistent lock sets, all n n are flagged as racing, yet Angr’s VEX IR ( shoshitaishvili2016sok ) cannot distinguish security-relevant manipulations from benign copies. R/R pair filtering (E4) removes 18 false positives (189 → \to 171) but eliminates 1 true positive (32 → \to 31), confirming that even the most conservative heuristic incurs true-positive losses inherent to exhaustive pair enumeration. Incomplete recall stems from three structural limitations of static binary analysis on WinRT COM binaries: WinRT’s produce<T,I> template breaks taint propagation through branchless this -pointer adjustments, virtual dispatch through stack-allocated structs requires points-to analysis beyond flat taint tracking, and partially-recovered statically-linked helpers cause missed field accesses ( § B.2 ). LLM-based reasoning recognizes these patterns semantically, maintaining perfect recall with GPT-5.4. Summary: Slyp achieves 3.3 × \times higher F1 than the best COMRace++ configuration (0.973 vs. 0.299). COMRace++ produces 171–189 false positives across configurations due to exhaustive pair enumeration. Incomplete recall (0.484–0.500) reflects structural limitations of static taint propagation on WinRT COM binaries that Slyp ’s semantic reasoning overcomes. 7. Discussion Domain-Specific Knowledge Requirements. LLMs lack comprehensive knowledge of platform-specific APIs and synchronization primitives, impacting analysis accuracy for race conditions involving critical sections, locks, and reference counting semantics. We address this through in-context learning ( brown2020language ; wei2022chain ) by incorporating detailed explanations of synchronization APIs, locking patterns, and COM lifecycle management into task prompts. This proves effective, as evidenced by 28 confirmed vulnerabilities discovered in production Windows services. A natural next step is domain-specific fine-tuning or knowledge augmentation to reduce prompt-engineering overhead ( lyu2024knowtuning ; meng2022locating ) . Decompilation and Symbols. Slyp leverages official Microsoft debug symbols to produce high-quality pseudocode via IDA Pro’s Hex-Rays decompiler. Like other systems ( han2023queryx ) , mis-inferred types or control flow can hide or fabricate bug patterns even with symbols. Slyp mitigates this through multiple decompilation views, LLM reasoning that tolerates artifacts, and vtable ...

TITLE: Agentic Vulnerability Reasoning on Windows COM Binaries

ABSTRACT: Windows Component Object Model (COM) services run with elevated privileges and are widely accessible to authenticated users, making race conditions in these binaries a critical surface for local privilege escalation. We present SLYP, an end-to-end agentic pipeline that discovers race condition vulnerabilities in COM binaries and generates debugger-verified proof-of-concept (PoC) code. SLYP exposes binary exploration, COM inspection, and dynamic debugging as reusable tool interfaces, giving agents the static context, COM activation metadata, and debugger feedback needed to move from vulnerability discovery to verified PoC generation. On a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases, SLYP achieves 0.973 F1, outperforming production coding agents by up to 0.208 F1 and the state-of-the-art static analyzer by 3.3x in bug discovery. For PoC generation, production coding agents in their default setup (without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools) verify essentially no cases on either frontier model, whereas SLYP's interactive toolsets enable it to autonomously synthesize working PoCs for 67.5% of cases on the strongest configuration. Deployed on production Windows services, SLYP discovers 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across nine COM services, all confirmed by the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) with 16 CVEs assigned and $140,000 in bounties. Furthermore, SLYP is designed with generalizable binary analysis and debugging interfaces, making it readily applicable to other commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries beyond Windows COM services.

INTRODUCTION: Windows Component Object Model (COM) services constitute a vast attack surface for local privilege escalation. Hundreds of privileged COM classes run in Windows service processes with elevated privileges, and many are accessible to authenticated users through Remote Procedure Call (RPC) ( microsoft2024com ; gu2022comrace ) . Race conditions in these services arise when concurrent RPC threads access shared object state without synchronization, producing use-after-free and double-free vulnerabilities that yield arbitrary code execution at the privilege level of the hosting service ( gu2022comrace ; upadhyay2023navigating ) . These vulnerabilities are prevalent across Windows installations yet remain difficult to detect. Analysis requires reasoning about thread interleavings, object lifetimes, and locking protocols simultaneously across functions that optimizing compilers strip of all type and symbol information. Existing approaches to detecting race conditions in COM binaries face fundamental limitations. Coverage-guided fuzzers lack control over thread scheduling to trigger specific interleavings ( jeong2019razzer ) . Static analyzers such as COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) rely on exhaustive pair enumeration that produces false positives from incorrect alias results and unmodeled input conditions, achieving only 0.299 F1 in our evaluation ( § 6.5 ). Structural assumptions about register usage and vtable layouts further miss vulnerabilities across diverse binary patterns. Manual reverse engineering requires decompiling each entry function, tracing shared-state accesses, and checking locking discipline across many functions, which does not scale to production COM services. In recent years, Large language models (LLMs) offer a complementary capability. LLMs demonstrate strong semantic reasoning over decompiled code, recovering variable names, data structures, and types from binaries ( xie2024resym ; xu2025gennm ) . Agentic systems extend this by scaffolding LLM reasoning with external tool invocation and iterative validation ( yao2022react ; yang2024swe ; wang2024openhands ) . Pioneering work demonstrates that LLM agents can discover real-world vulnerabilities that elude traditional fuzzers ( projectzero2024naptime ; projectzero2024bigsleep ) . The Model Context Protocol (MCP) ( anthropic2024mcp ) standardizes tool interfaces, enabling agents such as Codex ( codex2025 ) and Claude Code ( Cc ) ( claudecode2026 ) to invoke binary analysis tools autonomously. The missing piece is a structured pipeline that channels LLM reasoning toward vulnerability-specific analysis patterns and extends from discovery through automated proof-of-concept (PoC) generation. We present Slyp , the first end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and generating debugger-verified PoC programs. Slyp provides three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration, COM inspection, and debugging. Together they cover decompilation and virtual dispatch resolution, interface metadata and activation templates, as well as live PoC compilation, execution, and crash capture. The agent follows a standard ReAct loop ( yao2022react ) with engineering features purpose-built for long-horizon binary analysis. These include pre-compaction checkpointing that preserves cross-function findings across context compression and task verification that prevents premature termination. The pipeline is model-agnostic and works across GPT and Claude models without modification. We evaluate Slyp on discovery and PoC generation against production coding agents ( Codex and Cc ) across six backbone models with identical task prompts. On a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases, Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 (GPT-5.4), while production agents with default minimal binary analysis tools reach 0.910 ( Codex , GPT-5.4) and 0.724 ( Cc , Opus 4.6). Augmenting production agents with all binary exploration tools narrows but does not close the gap, with Slyp still leading on every tested model by up to +0.208 F1. Moreover, Slyp also outperforms COMRace++ (our enhanced reproduction of the state-of-the-art static analyzer COMRace ( gu2022comrace ) ) by 3.3 × \times (0.973 vs. 0.299). On PoC generation, production agents without our COM inspection and dynamic debugging tools solve essentially no cases, while the best Slyp configuration solves 67.5% (27/40) and the four top configurations collectively solve 34 of 40. Deployed on production Windows services, Slyp has discovered 28 previously unknown vulnerabilities across 9 COM services. MSRC has confirmed all 28 vulnerabilities with 16 CVEs and awarded $140,000 in bounties. This paper makes the following contributions: • We build three reusable MCP tool interfaces for binary exploration ( § 4.3 ), COM inspection ( § 4.4 ), and dynamic debugging ( § 4.5 ) that integrate with any MCP-compatible agent and are the primary contributors to both vulnerability discovery and PoC generation. • We design and implement Slyp , an end-to-end agentic pipeline that combines a ReAct loop with engineering features for long-horizon binary analysis, outperforming production coding agents on every tested model for vulnerability discovery ( § 6.3 ) and PoC generation ( § 6.4 ). While this paper primarily focuses on Windows COM binaries, Slyp can be easily generalized to other commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries. • We construct a benchmark of 20 COM objects covering 40 vulnerability cases with manually-verified ground-truth labels, including 8 independently discovered 1-day CVEs for circularity-free validation, and provide agent trajectory artifacts across three scaffolds and six backbone models ( § 6 ). • We demonstrate practical impact through the discovery of 28 zero-day vulnerabilities across 9 production Windows services, all confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs assigned and bounties totaling $140,000 ( § 6.2 ).

DISCUSSION: Domain-Specific Knowledge Requirements. LLMs lack comprehensive knowledge of platform-specific APIs and synchronization primitives, impacting analysis accuracy for race conditions involving critical sections, locks, and reference counting semantics. We address this through in-context learning ( brown2020language ; wei2022chain ) by incorporating detailed explanations of synchronization APIs, locking patterns, and COM lifecycle management into task prompts. This proves effective, as evidenced by 28 confirmed vulnerabilities discovered in production Windows services. A natural next step is domain-specific fine-tuning or knowledge augmentation to reduce prompt-engineering overhead ( lyu2024knowtuning ; meng2022locating ) . Decompilation and Symbols. Slyp leverages official Microsoft debug symbols to produce high-quality pseudocode via IDA Pro’s Hex-Rays decompiler. Like other systems ( han2023queryx ) , mis-inferred types or control flow can hide or fabricate bug patterns even with symbols. Slyp mitigates this through multiple decompilation views, LLM reasoning that tolerates artifacts, and vtable resolution that provides ground-truth virtual dispatch targets ( xie2024resym ; shang2024far ; jiang2025beyond ) . Context Window Scaling. Stage 1 discovery completes within current 400K–1M+ token context windows, but Stage 2 PoC generation involves iterative compile-execute-debug cycles that consume 7.7–11.4M tokens per case on the strongest configurations ( Appendix D ). Slyp ’s pre-compaction checkpointing ( § 4.2 ) manages this overhead, contributing to 22–61% token reductions on Claude backbones relative to the production agent. Larger or more complex targets, such as analyzing hundreds of COM objects or tracing cross-binary dependencies, would exceed current limits. Recent work confirms that increasing context length degrades LLM performance even with sufficient retrieval capacity ( du2025context ; hong2025context ) , motivating structured management approaches that decouple reasoning depth from the raw token budget available to the model. Model Scaling and Task Decomposition. The RQ2 results reveal an inverse relationship between model capability and scaffold benefit, with the largest gap on Haiku 4.5 (+0.208 F1) and smallest on GPT-5.3-codex (+0.003). Task verification compensates for weaker reasoning by preventing premature termination, while stronger models partially self-organize without explicit scaffolding. This matters for cost-sensitive deployment, as Slyp makes smaller models viable for vulnerability reasoning that would otherwise require frontier-class capabilities, and the complementarity analysis ( § 6.4 ) suggests that ensembling across cost tiers can increase coverage beyond any single configuration. Generalization and Limitations. Adapting Slyp to a different vulnerability class or platform requires modifying the task prompt and potentially adding new MCP tools, though the binary exploration server and agent architecture (pre-compaction checkpointing, task verification) are fully reusable. Limitations include dependence on decompiler quality, non-determinism in LLM reasoning (addressed via the best-of-3 protocol), and the inherent difficulty of triggering race conditions due to timing-sensitive crash behavior. The six unsolved PoC cases involve complicated threading object acquisitions where agents must coordinate multiple concurrent operations to trigger the race condition.

LIMITATIONS: COMRace++ ’s limited base-configuration performance (0.225 F1, 0.500 recall, and 32 missed vulnerable functions out of 64) stems from four structural limitations that persist despite our engineering enhancements. Field-scoped taint origin. COMRace++ tracks shared state exclusively through this -relative field offsets, as taint propagation originates from the this pointer passed via rcx . Race conditions arising from global variables or other non-instance shared memory fall outside this taint scope entirely. C14 exemplifies this gap. All eight vulnerable functions involve unsynchronized access to global variables, and COMRace++ achieves F1 = 0 across all three configurations ( Table 7 ). Slyp detects all eight with every backbone model except Haiku 4.5, as LLM-based reasoning naturally considers all forms of shared mutable state regardless of provenance. WinRT produce<T,I> wrapper. WinRT’s produce<T,I> template wraps every interface method with a branchless null-safe this -pointer adjustment: lea rax,[rcx-8]; neg rcx; sbb rcx,rcx; and rcx,rax . The expression (a1 - 8) & -(int64)(a1 != 0) adjusts the interface pointer back to the implementation object when non-null. The taint engine’s binary operation handler for And can partially recover the (’this’, -8) value from the lea operand, but recursive member analysis expects rcx == (’this’, 0) . A value of (’this’, -8) does not satisfy this check, so the callee’s field accesses are unattributed. Different produce<> instantiations use different offsets ( this-8 , this-0x10 , this-0x18 ) depending on the interface’s position in the inheritance hierarchy. Allowing arbitrary offsets and arithmetic operations on pointer values as valid this aliases without type information would create false field-offset mappings across unrelated interfaces and sharply raise the false-positive rate. Indirect field access through struct construction. A further class of false negatives occurs when a method embeds the this pointer into a locally constructed struct and passes that struct to a transaction or callback mechanism. When this is stored into a stack-allocated struct ( v12[1] = this ) and the struct’s address is passed to a callee via rcx , the taint tracker sees rcx = (’rsp’, 0x40) , a stack pointer rather than a this -derived value. Recovering the relationship requires points-to analysis: tracking which memory locations contain pointers to which objects. The current engine tracks (’base’, offset) tuples through registers and direct stack slots but does not model struct-embedded pointer indirection. Statically-linked internal helpers. Statically-linked internal helper functions ( e . g . std::wstring constructors compiled into the binary) may be recovered only partially by lightweight static analysis. When the callee’s internal CFG or IR is incomplete, field accesses performed inside the helper can be missed during callee analysis. This primarily affects Write detection in our implementation, because confirming a write requires recoverable internal basic blocks that the fast CFG recovery does not produce, whereas Read accesses can still be conservatively approximated.

CONCLUSION: We present Slyp , the first end-to-end agentic pipeline for discovering race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and generating debugger-verified PoC programs without source code access. Slyp achieves 0.973 F1 on 20 COM objects, while production coding agents with default minimal tools reach 0.724–0.910, and state-of-the-art static analyzer COMRace++ achieves only 0.299. The specialized toolsets also benefit existing production agents, with augmenting them from minimal to full binary exploration improving discovery F1 by up to 0.214 and adding COM inspection with dynamic debugging increasing verified PoC cases by 1.7–2.0 × \times . On production Windows services, Slyp discovers 28 zero-day vulnerabilities across 9 COM services, confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs assigned and $140,000 in bounties.
