Reproduction methodology. We reproduce COMRace using IDA Python and Angr ( angrsite ) , implementing virtual function recovery, taint propagation, and field usage detection for read / write / free operations. The original vtable recovery algorithm assumes vtable addresses are loaded exclusively via the rcx register. Our dataset contains diverse vtable loading patterns across multiple registers, rendering the original assumptions inapplicable. Since vulnerability detection depends entirely on successful virtual function recovery, the vanilla implementation fails completely on our dataset. We extend the recovery logic to handle diverse register usage patterns and additional edge cases in production binaries, yielding COMRace++ . Validation. We validate COMRace++ against the original COMRace datasets, confirming that it produces field usage patterns consistent with the original paper and detects the vulnerabilities reported in that work.

Algorithm 2 presents the virtual function call recovery procedure, which resolves indirect calls through vtable pointers to their concrete target functions. This recovery is partial because backward object-provenance tracing may fail to resolve a vtable when the definition chain is broken by indirect assignments, cross-module calls, or compiler-driven register reuse. Algorithm Overview. The algorithm processes all call instructions in a function (Lines 3 – 17 ), filtering for virtual calls and resolving each to concrete function targets. The core workflow consists of five key operations implemented through specialized helper functions. Virtual Call Detection. CollectCalls extracts all call instructions from the function (Line 1 ), then IsVirtualCall identifies virtual calls by analyzing instruction patterns (Line 5 ). Virtual calls exhibit the characteristic pattern of indirect calls through vtable pointers with fixed offsets, distinguishing them from other indirect calls such as function pointer invocations. Object Provenance Tracing. For each confirmed virtual call, TraceObject identifies the object used at the call site by analyzing register usage and stack variables (Line 6 ). FollowObjectChain then traces this object backward through its definition chain, analyzing cross-references to identify constructor invocations and vtable assignments (Line 9 ). This backward analysis collects candidate vtables that the object might reference at runtime, handling polymorphic scenarios where multiple derived classes are possible. Method Resolution. ParseMethodOffset extracts the method offset from the call instruction by analyzing the vtable dereference pattern (Line 10 ). LookupVtable then reads the vtable structure at each candidate address, retrieving the function pointer at the computed offset (Lines 12 – 15 ). When multiple candidate vtables exist, all possible targets are collected, providing agents with complete information about potential polymorphic dispatch targets. Integration with Decompilation. The resolved mappings ℛ \mathcal{R} integrate into the decompilation pipeline, where resolved calls appear as comments in the pseudocode output formatted as // Resolved virtual call: function_name at 0xaddress . This annotation enables agents to understand complete call chains without manual vtable analysis. We analyze tool invocation patterns across all 12 agent-model configurations from the Stage 2 PoC generation evaluation ( § 6.4 ). Across 480 case-executions (12 configurations × \times 40 cases), agents invoke 39,334 MCP tool calls (81.9 per case on average) spanning tools from all three MCP servers in Table 1 . We group tools into Binary Exploration (decompilation, disassembly, cross-references), COM Inspection (interface discovery, proxy definitions, skeleton generation), and Dynamic Debugging (PoC submission, debugger control, process management). Table 9. Tool call distribution across three MCP server categories for all 12 PoC generation configurations. Calls : total tool invocations in the category. % : share of all tool calls for that configuration. The remaining share (not shown) corresponds to agent-native calls (file I/O, task management). Category distribution. As shown in Table 9 , Dynamic Debugging dominates across all configurations, accounting for 53.2% of all MCP tool calls (20,921 calls). This reflects the iterative compile-execute-debug cycle central to PoC generation, where agents repeatedly submit PoC variants via submit_poc (3,466 calls total, 7.2 per case), attach debuggers, inspect crash states, and resume execution. Binary Exploration accounts for 29.7% (11,678 calls), driven by search_functions (5,279 calls) and decompile_function_addr/name (3,885 calls) as agents analyze target functions and trace vulnerable code paths. COM Inspection accounts for 17.1% (6,735 calls), with get_proxy_definition (1,717 calls) and find_interface (1,527 calls) as the most frequent tools for resolving COM method signatures. Agent comparison. Slyp invokes more Binary Exploration tools than baselines (33.9% vs. 25.4% for Codex and 23.5% for Cc ), reflecting its structured approach of analyzing vulnerable function internals before writing PoC code. On a per-case basis, Slyp calls decompile_function_addr/name 10.1 times per case versus 6.1 for baselines, and search_functions 13.6 times versus 8.4. Cc allocates a larger share to Dynamic Debugging (61.6%) with proportionally fewer Binary Exploration calls, indicating a trial-and-error strategy that submits PoC variants earlier and relies on crash feedback to iteratively converge on the correct trigger. Role of skeleton code. The generate_skeleton_code tool produces compilable C++ templates that instantiate a target COM object and invoke its interface methods with correct GUIDs, threading model, and activation boilerplate. All 12 configurations invoke this tool, totaling 744 calls (1.6 per case on average), confirming that every agent relies on it as a foundational step. Without this tool, the agent must manually construct COM activation code, a process that requires resolving CLSIDs, interface IIDs, apartment threading setup ( CoInitializeEx ), and server-specific security attributes. Prior work on COM vulnerability exploitation assumes manually authored harness code ( gu2022comrace ) , and no existing binary analysis framework provides automated COM harness generation. The generate_skeleton_code tool eliminates this manual effort and enables agents to focus on the vulnerability-specific race condition trigger logic rather than COM plumbing, a prerequisite for fully automated PoC generation at scale.

Table 10 lists the knowledge cutoff dates, public release dates, and context window sizes of all evaluated models. The knowledge cutoff date indicates the latest date through which a model’s pre-training data extends. The latest knowledge cutoff across all models is August 2025 (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-mini, GPT-5.3-codex, and Sonnet 4.6). Table 10. Knowledge cutoff dates, release dates, and context window sizes of evaluated models. All zero-day patches were disclosed after the latest knowledge cutoff (August 2025). Twelve of the 20 COM objects in the evaluation dataset contain zero-day vulnerabilities initially discovered during Slyp ’s research campaign ( § 6.2 ). A potential concern is that if the ground truth were derived from Slyp ’s own output, any vulnerabilities Slyp fails to detect would be excluded by construction, artificially inflating recall. The dataset includes 8 COM objects containing independently discovered 1-day vulnerabilities that Slyp never encountered during development ( § 6.1.2 ). On this subset, Slyp achieves an average F1 of 0.943 across all six models, compared to 0.912 on the full 20-case dataset ( § 6.3 ). The comparable or higher performance on cases Slyp never analyzed confirms that circularity does not inflate the reported metrics.

Simplified code snippet for CVE-2025-53802. We present a detailed analysis of CVE-2025-53802 ( 21 ), a use-after-free vulnerability discovered by Slyp . The vulnerability arises from the interaction between put_ScanResponsePayload and get_ScanResponsePayload that operate on the same COM reference pointer at offset 192, shown in Figure 5 . put_ScanResponsePayload updates this pointer via operator= , which sequentially increments the new object’s reference count, reads the current pointer, stores the new pointer, and decrements the old object’s reference count, all without synchronization. Concurrently, get_ScanResponsePayload reads the same pointer and calls InternalAddRef using a virtual AddRef_Proxy . When two setter threads run while a third executes the getter, both setters decrement the same old object’s reference count to zero and free it. The getter then dereferences a freed pointer during InternalAddRef , causing a use-after-free that enables local privilege escalation from Low to System integrity. Slyp performance. Slyp successfully identifies this vulnerability with precise localization through LLM-based semantic reasoning. Although Slyp cannot recover the complete virtual function table due to cross-module dependencies, the LLM agent infers the functionality of InternalAddRef and InternalRelease based on their names and calling context. By recognizing these as standard COM reference counting operations, Slyp correctly determines that they manipulate shared object state without synchronization and pinpoints the vulnerable access within InternalAddRef where the freed object is dereferenced. The agent then synthesizes the precise interleaving scenario (two put_ScanResponsePayload calls followed by one get_ScanResponsePayload call) that triggers the race window, providing detailed thread interaction patterns that enable efficient verification. Comparison with COMRace . COMRace fails to recover two main methods due to incomplete vtable resolution, resulting in zero detection. COMRace++ , our improved reproduction with extended vtable recovery ( Appendix B ), successfully recovers both methods and flags them as vulnerable to race conditions, achieving perfect recall on this case. However, COMRace++ fails to resolve the virtual calls within InternalRelease and InternalAddRef , which breaks propagation to reference-count functions and contributes to missed localization of the true unsafe site. As a result, it reports line 7 of get_ScanResponsePayload (a benign copy operation for this+192 ) as the vulnerable point, missing the actual vulnerability within InternalAddRef where freed objects are accessed. Given the granularity at which VEX IR represents memory effects, precisely trimming benign expressions/statements without suppressing security-relevant accesses is challenging, as discussed in § 6.5 . Mitigating this requires extensive engineering to tune filters and heuristics that separate benign patterns from genuine vulnerabilities, underscoring the specialization burden. In contrast, Slyp ’s semantic reasoning distinguishes the benign field copy from the critical freed-object access, demonstrating superior precision in vulnerability localization.

sections.discussion: Long-horizon LLM agents depend on intermediate information-gathering turns, yet training feedback is usually observed only at the final answer, because process-level rewards require high-quality human annotation. Existing turn-level shaping methods reward turns that increase the likelihood of a gold answer, but they require answer supervision or stable task-specific verifiers. Conversely, label-free RL methods extract self-signals from output distributions, but mainly at the answer or trajectory level and therefore cannot assign credit to intermediate turns. We propose Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP), which treats semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcome states for potential-based turn-level credit assignment. For each query, SIOP samples multiple rollouts, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, and builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states. It then rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. The objective generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without task-specific gold verifiers while avoiding the broadcasted rollout-level advantages used by standard GRPO. We formalize the framework, characterize its supervised gold-answer limit, and show that SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks while approaching a gold-supervised outcome baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/dl-m9/SIOP.git.

full_text:
TITLE: Self-Induced Outcome Potential: Turn-Level Credit Assignment for Agents without Verifiers

ABSTRACT: Long-horizon LLM agents depend on intermediate information-gathering turns, yet training feedback is usually observed only at the final answer, because process-level rewards require high-quality human annotation. Existing turn-level shaping methods reward turns that increase the likelihood of a gold answer, but they require answer supervision or stable task-specific verifiers. Conversely, label-free RL methods extract self-signals from output distributions, but mainly at the answer or trajectory level and therefore cannot assign credit to intermediate turns. We propose Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP), which treats semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcome states for potential-based turn-level credit assignment. For each query, SIOP samples multiple rollouts, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, and builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states. It then rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. The objective generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without task-specific gold verifiers while avoiding the broadcasted rollout-level advantages used by standard GRPO. We formalize the framework, characterize its supervised gold-answer limit, and show that SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks while approaching a gold-supervised outcome baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/dl-m9/SIOP.git.

DISCUSSION: Long-horizon LLM agents depend on intermediate information-gathering turns, yet training feedback is usually observed only at the final answer, because process-level rewards require high-quality human annotation. Existing turn-level shaping methods reward turns that increase the likelihood of a gold answer, but they require answer supervision or stable task-specific verifiers. Conversely, label-free RL methods extract self-signals from output distributions, but mainly at the answer or trajectory level and therefore cannot assign credit to intermediate turns. We propose Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP), which treats semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcome states for potential-based turn-level credit assignment. For each query, SIOP samples multiple rollouts, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, and builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states. It then rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. The objective generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without task-specific gold verifiers while avoiding the broadcasted rollout-level advantages used by standard GRPO. We formalize the framework, characterize its supervised gold-answer limit, and show that SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks while approaching a gold-supervised outcome baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/dl-m9/SIOP.git.

sections.introduction: Gaussian Processes (GPs) offer a principled and flexible Bayesian nonparametric framework for learning unknown functions (Williams and Rasmussen, 2006 ) . Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) extend this framework to model correlations between output functions, proving effective across domains ranging from medical time-series prediction (Cheng et al. , 2020 ) to dynamical system modelling (Tang et al. , 2025 ) . However, standard MOGP models incur a cubic computational cost with respect to the number of outputs P P . This constitutes a bottleneck for high-dimensional applications such as climate modelling and spatial transcriptomics, where P P can easily reach the order of thousands (Bonilla et al. , 2007a ; Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) . Figure 1 : Schematic overview of the proposed T-LVMOGP framework. Inducing points 𝐙 \mathbf{Z} are placed in the embedding space. To mitigate this complexity, existing research largely focuses on imposing structural constraints, potentially sacrificing expressiveness for scalability. For instance, low-rank approaches assume output functions are linear combinations of shared latent functions (Wackernagel, 2003 ; Higdon et al. , 2008 ; Xing et al. , 2016 ; Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) . While computationally efficient, these linear assumptions often lack the expressiveness required to model intricate inter-output dependencies. For tensor-based observations, the outputs can be shaped into a multidimensional array so that the Kronecker and tensor algebra are exploited for efficient computation (Zhe et al. , 2019 ; Li et al. , 2020 ) . However, this approach is predicated on rigid structural assumptions, which are frequently violated in real-world scenarios. Dai et al. ( 2017 ) introduce a latent variable for each output, and construct separable kernels for MOGP. Jiang et al. ( 2025 ) generalise this idea to sum-of-separable kernels, and they apply stochastic variational inference techniques (Hoffman et al. , 2013 ; Hensman et al. , 2013 ) to the output space, facilitating better scalability with respect to an increasing number of outputs. However, their expressivity remains bound by the fixed structural properties of the kernel class. In this work, we propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), illustrated in Figure 1 , a framework that scales MOGPs to large output dimensions while preserving high expressiveness. To this end, we introduce a multi-output deep kernel that captures intricate correlations among outputs without imposing rigid structural constraints. We associate each output with a latent variable, serving as a compact representation (Bonilla et al. , 2007b ) . To ensure high expressiveness, we employ a neural network to map the concatenated input-latent representations into an embedding space, where correlations are defined (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2007 ; Calandra et al. , 2016 ; Wilson et al. , 2016b ) . This architecture enables seamless integration with existing scalable GP frameworks to handle a large output space. We employ the Sparse Variational GP (SVGP) framework (Titsias, 2009 ) , placing inducing points in the embedding space and performing stochastic variational inference following Hensman et al. ( 2013 ) . Furthermore, recently proposed tighter variational bounds (Titsias, 2025 ; Bui et al. , 2025 ) can be readily incorporated into this framework, which potentially enhances predictive performance. While deep kernels provide the requisite flexibility, naive implementations suffer from overfitting (Ober et al. , 2021 ) . In this work, we regularise the neural network via residual connections (He et al. , 2016 ) and spectral normalisation (Miyato et al. , 2018 ) . This encourages the transformation to be Lipschitz continuous, and its distance awareness promotes locally smooth and globally stable mappings. This is critical for the reliable performance of the multi-output deep kernel proposed in this work. Contributions This work makes the following contributions: (1) We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP, a scalable framework for building flexible multi-output deep kernels that scale to high-dimensional outputs while preserving rich cross-output dependencies. (2) To improve generalisation, we adopt a Lipschitz continuous neural network using residual connections and spectral normalisation. (3) We develop a stochastic variational inference procedure within the SVGP framework, supporting mini-batch training over both inputs and outputs. Our formulation naturally accommodates non-Gaussian likelihoods and tighter variational bounds. (4) Empirical experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms MOGP baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.
sections.discussion: Transformed Latent Variable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes: Supplementary Materials Xiaoyu Jiang Xinxing Shi Sokratia Georgaka Magnus Rattray Mauricio A Álvarez Abstract Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) provide a principled probabilistic framework for modelling correlated outputs but face scalability bottlenecks when applied to datasets with high-dimensional output spaces. To maintain tractability, existing methods typically resort to restrictive assumptions, such as employing low-rank or sum-of-separable kernels, which can limit expressiveness. We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), a novel framework that scales MOGPs to a massive number of outputs while preserving the capacity to capture meaningful inter-output dependencies. T-LVMOGP constructs a flexible multi-output deep kernel by mapping inputs and output-specific latent variables into an embedding space using a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Combined with stochastic variational inference, our model effectively scales to high-dimensional output settings. Across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with over 10 , 000 10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics data, T-LVMOGP outperforms baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. This encourages the transformation to be Lipschitz continuous, and its distance awareness promotes locally smooth and globally stable mappings. This is critical for the reliable performance of the multi-output deep kernel proposed in this work. Contributions This work makes the following contributions: (1) We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP, a scalable framework for building flexible multi-output deep kernels that scale to high-dimensional outputs while preserving rich cross-output dependencies. (2) To improve generalisation, we adopt a Lipschitz continuous neural network using residual connections and spectral normalisation. (3) We develop a stochastic variational inference procedure within the SVGP framework, supporting mini-batch training over both inputs and outputs. Our formulation naturally accommodates non-Gaussian likelihoods and tighter variational bounds. (4) Empirical experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms MOGP baselines in ...
sections.limitations: In the current formulation, we employ a mean-field approximation for the variational distribution of the latent variables, factorising q ​ ( 𝐇 ) q(\mathbf{H}) across outputs to ensure computational efficiency. While this enhances scalability, it may limit posterior expressiveness by ignoring potential coupling among outputs. Future work could address this trade-off by adopting structured variational distributions (Mishkin et al. , 2018 ; Tomczak et al. , 2020 ) , which capture complex dependencies without incurring prohibitive computational costs. Furthermore, although the parameter complexity of q ​ ( 𝐇 ) q(\mathbf{H}) scales linearly with the number of outputs P P , future work could investigate imposing additional constraints within the latent space, such as amortisation (Kingma and Welling, 2014 ) . Such constraints would reduce the parameterisation required for estimation, thereby further improving scalability for high-dimensional output spaces.
sections.conclusion: In this work, we introduce a new MOGP framework that alleviates the computational bottleneck induced by high-dimensional outputs while retaining sufficient modelling capacity to capture rich inter-output dependencies. Central to our approach is a Lipschitz-regularised multi-output deep kernel that reformulates MOGPs as scalar GPs defined on a learned embedding space. This transformation enables a direct reuse of scalable inference methods developed for single-output GPs, thereby yielding scalability with respect to the number of outputs. We instantiate the proposed model within the SVGP framework, considering both standard and tighter variational bounds. Future work includes integrating other scalability techniques like structured kernel interpolation (Wilson and Nickisch, 2015 ) and nearest-neighbour approximations (Tran et al. , 2021 ; Wu et al. , 2022 ) . Our model relies on a factorised variational distribution for latent variables, which scales linearly with the number of outputs but limits the capture of posterior output couplings. Future work could address this via structured variational distributions (Mishkin et al. , 2018 ) or amortised inference (Kingma and Welling, 2014 ) .

In this section, we relate the kernel structure of T-LVMOGP to that of prior Latent Variable MOGP models (Dai et al. , 2017 ; Jiang et al. , 2025 ) . We focus on the (ARD)-RBF kernels used throughout the paper.

To ensure a fair comparison, we implement all models, including the proposed T-LVMOGP and the baselines (Ind-GP, SV-LMC, SGPRN, OILMM, G-MOGP, and GS-LVMOGP), using the PyTorch (Paszke et al. , 2019 ) and GPyTorch (Gardner et al. , 2018 ) frameworks. Experiments are conducted on a high-performance computing cluster utilising NVIDIA HGX A100-SXM4 or NVIDIA L40S-SXM4 GPUs, with all computations performed in double precision ( Float64 ). For computational efficiency assessment, training times are benchmarked on an NVIDIA RTX-5090 GPU. Throughout this work, we employ the ARD-RBF kernel as the base kernel of T-LVMOGP for simplicity; and J = 1 J=1 Monte Carlo sample is adopted for model training. In terms of tighter variational bounds, the correction term Δ \Delta given by Equation 1 is evaluated conditionally on sampled 𝐡 p ( j ) \mathbf{h}_{p}^{(j)} . When data are missing, we denote Ω ⊆ { 1 , … , N } × { 1 , … , P } \Omega\subseteq\{1,...,N\}\times\{1,...,P\} as the index set for training data with | Ω | = N train |\Omega|=N_{\text{train}} . We draw input-output pairs from Ω \Omega and scale the mini-batch expected log-likelihood term in ℒ ~ 3 \widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{3} by N train / ( N ​ P ) N_{\text{train}}/(NP) , where N train < N ​ P N_{\text{train}}<NP denotes the total number of observed training data, to ensure an unbiased weight for the expected log-likelihood term. We choose S = 20 S=20 samples for latent variables when making predictions on the test set. The NLL is computed as follows: NLL = − log ⁡ q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐱 ∗ ) = − log ​ ∫ q ​ ( 𝐡 p ∗ ) ​ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 𝐡 p ∗ ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) \displaystyle=-\log q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{x}_{*})=-\log\int q(\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}})q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,d\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}}\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}) ≈ − log ⁡ 1 S ​ ∑ s ∫ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) \displaystyle\approx-\log\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s}\int q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}) = log ⁡ S − logsumexp s = 1 , … , S [ log ⁡ ∫ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ⏟ q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ] . \displaystyle=\log S-\operatorname*{logsumexp}_{s=1,...,S}\left[\log\underbrace{\int q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})}_{q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})}\right]. For the Gaussian likelihood, the integral for q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}) can be analytically solved. For non-Gaussian likelihoods, we approximate it by plugging in the mean value of q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}) , i.e. q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ≈ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f mean* ) , where ​ f mean* ​ represents the mean value of ​ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) . q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})\approx p(y_{*}\mid f_{\text{mean*}}),\;\text{where }f_{\text{mean*}}\text{ represents the mean value of }q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}).

There are more works about MOGPs in the literature. Yu et al. ( 2005 ) exploit the equivalence between parametric linear models and GPs for multi-task learning in a hierarchical Bayesian framework. Semiparametric Latent Factor Models (SLFM) can be viewed as a special case of LMC (Teh et al. , 2005 ) . Latent Force Models (LFMs) (Alvarez et al. , 2009 ) induce dependencies by passing latent GP forces through mechanistic differential equations, yielding physically interpretable multi-output kernels. Melkumyan and Ramos ( 2011 ) develop multi-task covariance functions by combining stationary kernels via Fourier arguments under the process convolution framework. Collaborative MOGP (Nguyen and Bonilla, 2014 ) represents output functions as a combination of two elements: projections from latent GPs shared across all outputs and individual GPs capturing the output-specific residuals. Kaiser et al. ( 2018 ) propose a hierarchical MOGP model, where Convolution Process layers are embedded in the middle of Deep Gaussian Processes (DGP) (Damianou and Lawrence, 2013 ) for explicitly modelling output dependencies. To enhance expressivity in multichannel signal modelling, the Multi-Output Spectral Mixture kernel (MOSM) (Parra and Tobar, 2017 ; Altamirano and Tobar, 2022 ) is introduced, which is effective for modelling phase differences and delays among outputs. García-Hinde et al. ( 2022 ) reformulate the MOGP problem as a sequence of conditioned univariate GPs by augmenting the input space with previous output values—a strategy similarly adopted in autoregressive GP modelling (Requeima et al. , 2019 ) . While MOGP literature predominantly focuses on modelling output correlations through complex covariance structures, Fortuin et al. ( 2019 ) demonstrate that meta-learning deep mean functions can be a competitive strategy for knowledge transfer in low-data regimes. In terms of applications and specific extensions, Zhao and Sun ( 2016 ) propose a convolved MOGP model for complex dynamical systems such as motion capture, traffic flow and robot inverse dynamics. Yousefi et al. ( 2019 ) construct an MOGP model based on LMC for modelling data points that have been aggregated at different input scales. To improve heterogeneous modelling of MOGP, Giraldo and Alvarez ( 2021 ) propose a fully natural gradient scheme applicable for both LMC and process convolution. López-Lopera et al. ( 2022 ) apply an MOGP model with a separable kernel to surrogate modelling of coastal flood hazard assessment. Yoon et al. ( 2022 ) introduce a specialised variant of LMC that decomposes input effects on outputs into components shared across or specific to tasks and samples. Ma and Álvarez ( 2023 ) introduce a multi-output multi-class classification model based on LMC. Ma et al. ( 2023 ) extend LV-MOGP for modelling hierarchical datasets, where several replicates are observed for each output.

We provide a comparative analysis of the baseline models (SV-LMC (Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) , SGPRN (Li et al. , 2020 ) , OILMM (Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) , G-MOGP (Dai et al. , 2024 ) , and GS-LVMOGP (Jiang et al. , 2025 ) ), focusing on their respective model sizes and computational costs based on our implementation. Notably, in the following evaluation, kernel parameters are excluded from the parameter enumeration.

In this section, we present a detailed review of the two scalable MOGP models adopted as baselines in this paper—namely, SGPRN (Li et al. , 2020 ) and OILMM (Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) —to provide interested readers with additional background and context.

This section provides a comparison between the T-LVMOGP and baseline models from a methodological perspective.
sections.raw_text: Transformed Latent Variable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes: Supplementary Materials Xiaoyu Jiang Xinxing Shi Sokratia Georgaka Magnus Rattray Mauricio A Álvarez Abstract Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) provide a principled probabilistic framework for modelling correlated outputs but face scalability bottlenecks when applied to datasets with high-dimensional output spaces. To maintain tractability, existing methods typically resort to restrictive assumptions, such as employing low-rank or sum-of-separable kernels, which can limit expressiveness. We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), a novel framework that scales MOGPs to a massive number of outputs while preserving the capacity to capture meaningful inter-output dependencies. T-LVMOGP constructs a flexible multi-output deep kernel by mapping inputs and output-specific latent variables into an embedding space using a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Combined with stochastic variational inference, our model effectively scales to high-dimensional output settings. Across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with over 10 , 000 10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics data, T-LVMOGP outperforms baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. Machine Learning, ICML 1 Introduction Gaussian Processes (GPs) offer a principled and flexible Bayesian nonparametric framework for learning unknown functions (Williams and Rasmussen, 2006 ) . Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) extend this framework to model correlations between output functions, proving effective across domains ranging from medical time-series prediction (Cheng et al. , 2020 ) to dynamical system modelling (Tang et al. , 2025 ) . However, standard MOGP models incur a cubic computational cost with respect to the number of outputs P P . This constitutes a bottleneck for high-dimensional applications such as climate modelling and spatial transcriptomics, where P P can easily reach the order of thousands (Bonilla et al. , 2007a ; Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) . Figure 1 : Schematic overview of the proposed T-LVMOGP framework. Inducing points 𝐙 \mathbf{Z} are placed in the embedding space. To mitigate this complexity, existing research largely focuses on imposing structural constraints, potentially sacrificing expressiveness for scalability. For instance, low-rank approaches assume output functions are linear combinations of shared latent functions (Wackernagel, 2003 ; Higdon et al. , 2008 ; Xing et al. , 2016 ; Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) . While computationally efficient, these linear assumptions often lack the expressiveness required to model intricate inter-output dependencies. For tensor-based observations, the outputs can be shaped into a multidimensional array so that the Kronecker and tensor algebra are exploited for efficient computation (Zhe et al. , 2019 ; Li et al. , 2020 ) . However, this approach is predicated on rigid structural assumptions, which are frequently violated in real-world scenarios. Dai et al. ( 2017 ) introduce a latent variable for each output, and construct separable kernels for MOGP. Jiang et al. ( 2025 ) generalise this idea to sum-of-separable kernels, and they apply stochastic variational inference techniques (Hoffman et al. , 2013 ; Hensman et al. , 2013 ) to the output space, facilitating better scalability with respect to an increasing number of outputs. However, their expressivity remains bound by the fixed structural properties of the kernel class. In this work, we propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), illustrated in Figure 1 , a framework that scales MOGPs to large output dimensions while preserving high expressiveness. To this end, we introduce a multi-output deep kernel that captures intricate correlations among outputs without imposing rigid structural constraints. We associate each output with a latent variable, serving as a compact representation (Bonilla et al. , 2007b ) . To ensure high expressiveness, we employ a neural network to map the concatenated input-latent representations into an embedding space, where correlations are defined (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2007 ; Calandra et al. , 2016 ; Wilson et al. , 2016b ) . This architecture enables seamless integration with existing scalable GP frameworks to handle a large output space. We employ the Sparse Variational GP (SVGP) framework (Titsias, 2009 ) , placing inducing points in the embedding space and performing stochastic variational inference following Hensman et al. ( 2013 ) . Furthermore, recently proposed tighter variational bounds (Titsias, 2025 ; Bui et al. , 2025 ) can be readily incorporated into this framework, which potentially enhances predictive performance. While deep kernels provide the requisite flexibility, naive implementations suffer from overfitting (Ober et al. , 2021 ) . In this work, we regularise the neural network via residual connections (He et al. , 2016 ) and spectral normalisation (Miyato et al. , 2018 ) . This encourages the transformation to be Lipschitz continuous, and its distance awareness promotes locally smooth and globally stable mappings. This is critical for the reliable performance of the multi-output deep kernel proposed in this work. Contributions This work makes the following contributions: (1) We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP, a scalable framework for building flexible multi-output deep kernels that scale to high-dimensional outputs while preserving rich cross-output dependencies. (2) To improve generalisation, we adopt a Lipschitz continuous neural network using residual connections and spectral normalisation. (3) We develop a stochastic variational inference procedure within the SVGP framework, supporting mini-batch training over both inputs and outputs. Our formulation naturally accommodates non-Gaussian likelihoods and tighter variational bounds. (4) Empirical experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms MOGP baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. 2 Background 2.1 Gaussian Processes Consider a regression problem with a dataset of N N input-output pairs ( 𝐗 , 𝐲 ) = { ( 𝐱 n , y n ) } n = 1 N (\mathbf{X},\mathbf{y})=\{(\mathbf{x}_{n},y_{n})\}_{n=1}^{N} , where 𝐗 ∈ ℝ N × D X \mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times D_{X}} and 𝐲 ∈ ℝ N \mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{N} . We assume the outputs are generated by a latent function f f with observation noise, y n = f ​ ( 𝐱 n ) + ϵ n y_{n}=f(\mathbf{x}_{n})+\epsilon_{n} . A zero-mean GP places a prior over the function f ∼ 𝒢 ​ 𝒫 ​ ( 0 , k ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ′ ) ) f\sim\mathcal{GP}(0,k(\cdot,\cdot^{\prime})) . The function values 𝐟 = f ​ ( 𝐗 ) \mathbf{f}=f(\mathbf{X}) follow a multivariate Gaussian distribution p ​ ( 𝐟 ) = 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝟎 , 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ) p(\mathbf{f})=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{0},\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{XX}}) , where [ 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ] i ​ j = k ​ ( 𝐱 i , 𝐱 j ) [\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{XX}}]_{ij}=k(\mathbf{x}_{i},\mathbf{x}_{j}) . Exact GPs incur an 𝒪 ​ ( N 3 ) \mathcal{O}(N^{3}) computational cost due to the determinant and inversion computation of an N × N N\times N matrix. To scale to large datasets, SVGP (Titsias, 2009 ) introduces a set of M ≪ N M\ll N inducing inputs 𝐙 ∈ ℝ M × D X \mathbf{Z}\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times D_{X}} and corresponding inducing variables 𝐮 = f ​ ( 𝐙 ) \mathbf{u}=f(\mathbf{Z}) . The joint posterior is approximated using a variational distribution q ​ ( f , 𝐮 ) = p ​ ( f ∣ 𝐮 ) ​ q ​ ( 𝐮 ) q(f,\mathbf{u})=p(f\mid\mathbf{u})q(\mathbf{u}) , where q ​ ( 𝐮 ) = 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐮 ∣ 𝐦 , 𝐒 ) q(\mathbf{u})=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{u}\mid\mathbf{m},\mathbf{S}) and 𝐦 , 𝐒 \mathbf{m},\mathbf{S} are variational parameters. We denote 𝐊 𝐙𝐙 = k ​ ( 𝐙 , 𝐙 ) \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ZZ}}=k(\mathbf{Z},\mathbf{Z}) and 𝐊 𝐗𝐙 = k ​ ( 𝐗 , 𝐙 ) \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{XZ}}=k(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{Z}) . The standard SVGP is trained by maximising the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO): ℒ 1 = ∑ n = 1 N 𝔼 q ​ ( f ​ ( 𝐱 n ) ) [ log p ( y n ∣ f ( 𝐱 n ) ) ] − KL [ q ( 𝐮 ) ∣ ∣ p ( 𝐮 ) ] . \mathcal{L}_{1}=\sum_{n=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}_{q(f(\mathbf{x}_{n}))}\left[\log p(y_{n}\mid f(\mathbf{x}_{n}))\right]-\operatorname{KL}[q(\mathbf{u})\mid\mid p(\mathbf{u})]. Recently, Titsias ( 2025 ) and Bui et al. ( 2025 ) propose a refined variational posterior that relaxes the prior conditional assumption. They define q ​ ( 𝐟 , 𝐮 ) = q ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝐮 ) ​ q ​ ( 𝐮 ) q(\mathbf{f},\mathbf{u})=q(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{u})q(\mathbf{u}) with q ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝐮 ) = 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝐊 𝐗𝐙 ​ 𝐊 𝐙𝐙 − 1 ​ 𝐮 , 𝐃 𝐗𝐗 1 / 2 ​ 𝐕𝐃 𝐗𝐗 ⊤ ⁣ / 2 ) , q(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{u})=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{Z}}\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{Z}}^{-1}\mathbf{u},\mathbf{D}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}^{1/2}\mathbf{V}\mathbf{D}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}^{\top/2}), where 𝐕 \mathbf{V} is a positive diagonal matrix, 𝐐 𝐗𝐗 = 𝐊 𝐗𝐙 ​ 𝐊 𝐙𝐙 − 1 ​ 𝐊 𝐙𝐗 \mathbf{Q}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}=\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{Z}}\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{Z}}^{-1}\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{X}} represents the Nyström approximation, and 𝐃 𝐗𝐗 = 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 − 𝐐 𝐗𝐗 \mathbf{D}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}=\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}-\mathbf{Q}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}} . This parameterisation leads to a tighter lower bound ℒ 2 = ℒ 1 + Δ \mathcal{L}_{2}=\mathcal{L}_{1}+\Delta after optimising out 𝐕 \mathbf{V} , where Δ ≥ 0 \Delta\geq 0 serves as a correction term. For the Gaussian likelihood with noise variance σ y 2 \sigma_{y}^{2} , this term is given analytically by: Δ = 1 2 ​ ∑ n = 1 N [ d n σ y 2 − log ⁡ ( 1 + d n σ y 2 ) ] , \Delta=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left[\frac{d_{n}}{\sigma_{y}^{2}}-\log\left(1+\frac{d_{n}}{\sigma_{y}^{2}}\right)\right], (1) where d n = [ 𝐃 𝐗𝐗 ] n ​ n d_{n}=[\mathbf{D}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}]_{nn} is the n n -th diagonal element of 𝐃 𝐗𝐗 \mathbf{D}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}} . Further details are provided in Appendix G . 2.2 Multi-Output Gaussian Processes MOGPs are a natural extension of GPs to vector-valued observations 𝐘 ∈ ℝ N × P \mathbf{Y}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times P} where 𝐲 n ∈ ℝ P \mathbf{y}_{n}\in\mathbb{R}^{P} denotes the observation at input 𝐱 n \mathbf{x}_{n} (Alvarez et al. , 2012 ) . A classic approach is the Linear Model of Coregionalisation (LMC), which expresses outputs as linear combinations of independent latent GPs (Journel and Huijbregts, 1976 ) . Specifically, the p p -th output function f p f_{p} is modelled as: f p ​ ( 𝐱 n ) = ∑ q = 1 Q ∑ r = 1 R q α p , r ( q ) ​ g r ( q ) ​ ( 𝐱 n ) , y n , p = f p ​ ( 𝐱 n ) + ϵ n , p , f_{p}(\mathbf{x}_{n})=\sum_{q=1}^{Q}\sum_{r=1}^{R_{q}}\alpha^{(q)}_{p,r}g^{(q)}_{r}(\mathbf{x}_{n}),\;y_{n,p}=f_{p}(\mathbf{x}_{n})+\epsilon_{n,p}, where ϵ n , p ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , σ y 2 ) \epsilon_{n,p}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{y}^{2}) , { α p , r ( q ) } \{\alpha^{(q)}_{p,r}\} are mixing weights and the functions { g r ( q ) ​ ( 𝐱 ) } r = 1 R q \{g^{(q)}_{r}(\mathbf{x})\}_{r=1}^{R_{q}} are latent GPs sharing covariance function k X ( q ) ​ ( 𝐱 , 𝐱 ′ ) k_{X}^{(q)}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}^{\prime}) , but sampled independently. The cross-covariance between f p ​ ( 𝐱 ) f_{p}(\mathbf{x}) and f p ′ ​ ( 𝐱 ′ ) f_{p^{\prime}}(\mathbf{x}^{\prime}) is given by cov ​ [ f p ​ ( 𝐱 ) , f p ′ ​ ( 𝐱 ′ ) ] \displaystyle\text{cov}[f_{p}(\mathbf{x}),f_{p^{\prime}}(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})] = ∑ q = 1 Q c p , p ′ ( q ) ​ k X ( q ) ​ ( 𝐱 , 𝐱 ′ ) , \displaystyle=\sum_{q=1}^{Q}c^{(q)}_{p,p^{\prime}}k_{X}^{(q)}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}^{\prime}), with c p , p ′ ( q ) = ∑ r = 1 R q α p , r ( q ) ​ α p ′ , r ( q ) c_{p,p^{\prime}}^{(q)}=\sum_{r=1}^{R_{q}}\alpha^{(q)}_{p,r}\alpha^{(q)}_{p^{\prime},r} . 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ( q ) = k X ( q ) ​ ( 𝐗 , 𝐗 ) \mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}=k_{X}^{(q)}(\mathbf{X},\mathbf{X}) is from the q q -th input kernel. Let 𝐟 = [ 𝐟 1 ⊤ , … , 𝐟 P ⊤ ] ⊤ ∈ ℝ P ​ N \mathbf{f}=[\mathbf{f}_{1}^{\top},\dots,\mathbf{f}_{P}^{\top}]^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{PN} be the vectorised latent function values with 𝐟 p = f p ​ ( 𝐗 ) \mathbf{f}_{p}=f_{p}(\mathbf{X}) . The covariance matrix for 𝐟 \mathbf{f} is cov ​ [ 𝐟 , 𝐟 ] = ∑ q = 1 Q 𝐀 ( q ) ​ 𝐀 ( q ) ⊤ ⊗ 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ( q ) = ∑ q = 1 Q 𝐂 ( q ) ⊗ 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ( q ) , \text{cov}[\mathbf{f},\mathbf{f}]=\sum_{q=1}^{Q}\mathbf{A}^{(q)}{\mathbf{A}^{(q)}}^{\top}\otimes\mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}=\sum_{q=1}^{Q}\mathbf{C}^{(q)}\otimes\mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}, where ⊗ \otimes denotes the Kronecker product, 𝐀 ( q ) ∈ ℝ P × R q \mathbf{A}^{(q)}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times R_{q}} collects α p , r ( q ) \alpha^{(q)}_{p,r} , and the coregionalisation matrix 𝐂 ( q ) ∈ ℝ P × P \mathbf{C}^{(q)}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times P} contains c p , p ′ ( q ) c^{(q)}_{p,p^{\prime}} . Notably, LMC implies that 𝐂 ( q ) \mathbf{C}^{(q)} is a low-rank matrix derived from a linear kernel applied to the latent output embeddings (the rows of 𝐀 ( q ) \mathbf{A}^{(q)} ). The Latent Variable MOGP (LV-MOGP) (Dai et al. , 2017 ; Jiang et al. , 2025 ) generalises this framework by assigning latent variables to each output and constructing 𝐂 ( q ) \mathbf{C}^{(q)} by applying any valid kernel function to them. This formulation enhances the model’s capacity to capture more complex output correlations. Let 𝐇 = { 𝐇 ( q ) } q = 1 Q \mathbf{H}=\{\mathbf{H}^{(q)}\}_{q=1}^{Q} denote the collection of latent variables, where 𝐇 ( q ) ∈ ℝ P × D H \mathbf{H}^{(q)}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times D_{H}} contains the latent vector 𝐡 p , q ∈ ℝ D H \mathbf{h}_{p,q}\in\mathbb{R}^{D_{H}} for the p p -th output in the q q -th group. We have 𝐂 ( q ) = 𝐊 𝐇 ( q ) ​ 𝐇 ( q ) ( q ) = k H ( q ) ​ ( 𝐇 ( q ) , 𝐇 ( q ) ) \mathbf{C}^{(q)}=\mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{H}^{(q)}\mathbf{H}^{(q)}}=k_{H}^{(q)}(\mathbf{H}^{(q)},\mathbf{H}^{(q)}) , where k H ( q ) k^{(q)}_{H} denotes the q q -th kernel function on the latent space. Under a Bayesian framework, generative distributions are p ​ ( 𝐡 p , q ) \displaystyle p\left(\mathbf{h}_{p,q}\right) = 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐡 p , q ∣ 𝟎 , 𝐈 ) , \displaystyle=\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{h}_{p,q}\mid\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I}\right), p ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝐗 , 𝐇 ) \displaystyle p\left(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{H}\right) = 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐟 ∣ 𝟎 , ∑ q = 1 Q 𝐊 𝐇 ( q ) ​ 𝐇 ( q ) ( q ) ⊗ 𝐊 𝐗𝐗 ( q ) ) , \displaystyle=\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{f}\mid\mathbf{0},\sum_{q=1}^{Q}\mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{H}^{(q)}\mathbf{H}^{(q)}}\otimes\mathbf{K}^{(q)}_{\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}}\right), p ​ ( vec ⁡ ( 𝐘 ) ∣ 𝐟 ) \displaystyle p\left(\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{Y})\mid\mathbf{f}\right) = 𝒩 ​ ( vec ⁡ ( 𝐘 ) ∣ 𝐟 , σ y 2 ​ 𝐈 ) , \displaystyle=\mathcal{N}\left(\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{Y})\mid\mathbf{f},\sigma_{y}^{2}\mathbf{I}\right), where vec ⁡ ( ⋅ ) \operatorname{vec}(\cdot) denotes the vectorisation operation. Scalable inference is achieved via a sparse variational method that places inducing points in both the input and latent spaces. For latent variables, LV-MOGP introduces variational distributions q ​ ( 𝐇 ) q(\mathbf{H}) ; unlike point estimation, this Bayesian treatment mitigates the risk of overfitting (Titsias and Lawrence, 2010 ; Lalchand et al. , 2022a ) . Figure 2 : Illustration of the proposed multi-output deep kernel with Lipschitz-regularised residual connected neural network (RCNN). SN: spectral normalisation. 2.3 Deep Kernels and Lipschitz Regularisation Deep Kernel Learning (Wilson et al. , 2016b , a ) unifies the representation power of neural networks with GPs by defining a kernel over learned embeddings. However, unconstrained neural networks can lead to pathological behaviour, such as feature collapse and the loss of distance awareness (Liu et al. , 2020 ; Ober et al. , 2021 ) . This degradation undermines the inherent benefits of GPs for quantifying uncertainty, leading to overconfident predictions on new data points (Ober et al. , 2021 ; Van Amersfoort et al. , 2021 ) . To mitigate this, Van Amersfoort et al. ( 2021 ) propose enforcing a Lipschitz constraint on the neural network, recovering the principled uncertainty of deep kernel GPs. In this work, we leverage these Lipschitz-regularised deep kernels with appropriate expressiveness and extend them to LV-MOGPs. 3 Transformed Latent Variable MOGP Building on LV-MOGP, our approach introduces a novel, expressive multi-output deep kernel that explicitly transforms complex MOGPs into more tractable scalar GPs in a learned embedding space. Formulated within the SVGP framework (Hensman et al. , 2013 ) , our model scales to datasets with high-dimensional outputs while effectively capturing essential inter-output correlations. 3.1 Model Definition Let 𝐇 = { 𝐡 p } p = 1 P ∈ ℝ P × D H \mathbf{H}=\{\mathbf{h}_{p}\}_{p=1}^{P}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times D_{H}} represent latent variables assigned to every output. In MOGP, the central modelling choice is the specification of the cross-output covariances, i.e. the construction of k p , p ′ ​ ( 𝐱 , 𝐱 ′ ) k_{p,p^{\prime}}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}^{\prime}) for p , p ′ ∈ { 1 , … , P } , 𝐱 , 𝐱 ′ ∈ 𝐗 p,p^{\prime}\in\{1,...,P\},\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\in\mathbf{X} . We build it in two stages: (1) map input and latent variable pairs to an embedding space, and (2) define covariances via a base kernel on that space. Concretely, we introduce a neural network Φ θ : ℝ D X × ℝ D H → ℝ D T \Phi_{\theta}:\mathbb{R}^{D_{X}}\times\mathbb{R}^{D_{H}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{D_{T}} that takes the concatenated pair ( 𝐱 n , 𝐡 p ) (\mathbf{x}_{n},\mathbf{h}_{p}) and returns an embedding 𝐱 ~ n , p ∈ ℝ D T \widetilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n,p}\in\mathbb{R}^{D_{T}} , where D T D_{T} is the dimensionality of the embedding space. Given a stationary kernel k base ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) k_{\text{base}}(\cdot,\cdot) on the embedding space (e.g., the RBF kernel), we define the cross-covariances as cov ​ [ f p ​ ( 𝐱 n ) , f p ′ ​ ( 𝐱 n ′ ) ] = k base ​ ( 𝐱 ~ n , p , 𝐱 ~ n ′ , p ′ ) . \text{cov}[f_{p}(\mathbf{x}_{n}),f_{p^{\prime}}(\mathbf{x}_{n^{\prime}})]=k_{\text{base}}(\widetilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n,p},\widetilde{\mathbf{x}}_{n^{\prime},p^{\prime}}). Figure 2 depicts the construction of our kernel. Notably, we do not impose restrictive assumptions, such as low-rank or the sum-of-separable structures, on our kernel. Instead, correlations are induced implicitly through a learned embedding and a base kernel. 1 1 1 Our kernel generalises the separable and sum-of-separable structures employed in existing LV-MOGP models, as detailed in Appendix D . As demonstrated in our experiments, this approach enables greater capacity in capturing intricate correlations among high-dimensional outputs. To mitigate the overfitting issues common in deep kernels (Ober et al. , 2021 ; Matias et al. , 2024 ) , we parameterise Φ θ \Phi_{\theta} as a Residual Connected Neural Network (RCNN) (He et al. , 2016 ) and apply spectral normalisation (Miyato et al. , 2018 ) to the weight matrices of each layer. Specifically, we adopt the power iteration algorithm (Golub and Van Loan, 2013 ) , as outlined in Algorithm 1 in Appendix C . By setting a proper Spectral Norm Upper Bound (SN-UB) of the weight matrices, Φ θ \Phi_{\theta} is Lipschitz continuous with the desired Lipschitz constant ( that is ​ ( 1 + SN-UB ) L ​ for ​ L ​ layer network ) \left(\text{that is }(1+\text{SN-UB})^{L}\text{ for }L\text{ layer network}\right) (Liu et al. , 2020 ) , which is demonstrated to be significant for achieving improved generalisation ability in our experiments. Moreover, this parameterisation offers the expressive capacity to model a wide range of smooth Lipschitz mappings, suggesting the versatility of this approach (Bartlett et al. , 2018 ) . Further details regarding Lipschitz continuity and spectral normalisation can be found in Appendix C . 3.2 Variational Inference We introduce prior and variational distributions for the latent variables 𝐇 \mathbf{H} across outputs: p ​ ( 𝐇 ) \displaystyle p(\mathbf{H}) = ∏ p = 1 P p ​ ( 𝐡 p ) = ∏ p = 1 P 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐡 p ∣ 0 , 𝐈 ) , \displaystyle=\prod_{p=1}^{P}p(\mathbf{h}_{p})=\prod_{p=1}^{P}\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{h}_{p}\mid 0,\mathbf{I}), q ​ ( 𝐇 ) \displaystyle q(\mathbf{H}) = ∏ p = 1 P q ​ ( 𝐡 p ) = ∏ p = 1 P 𝒩 ​ ( 𝐡 p ∣ 𝐦 p , 𝚺 p ) . \displaystyle=\prod_{p=1}^{P}q(\mathbf{h}_{p})=\prod_{p=1}^{P}\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{h}_{p}\mid\mathbf{m}_{p},\mathbf{\Sigma}_{p}). We compute the inner expectation 𝒱 n , p ​ ( 𝐡 p ) = 𝔼 q ​ ( f p ​ ( 𝐱 n ) ∣ 𝐱 n , 𝐡 p ) ​ [ log ⁡ p ​ ( y n , p ∣ f p ​ ( 𝐱 n ) ) ] \mathcal{V}_{n,p}(\mathbf{h}_{p})=\mathbb{E}_{q(f_{p}(\mathbf{x}_{n})\mid\mathbf{x}_{n},\mathbf{h}_{p})}[\log p(y_{n,p}\mid f_{p}(\mathbf{x}_{n}))] based on the likelihood type. (3) Since this integration is analytically intractable, we employ a Monte Carlo approximation. We draw S S samples from q ​ ( 𝐡 p ∗ ) q(\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}}) , approximating Equation 3 as a mixture of Gaussian distributions: q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ≈ 1 S ​ ∑ s = 1 S 𝒩 ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ μ ∗ ( s ) , σ ∗ 2 ( s ) ) , q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\approx\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\mathcal{N}(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mu_{*}^{(s)},{\sigma^{2}_{*}}^{(s)}), μ ∗ ( s ) \mu_{*}^{(s)} and σ ∗ 2 ( s ) {\sigma^{2}_{*}}^{(s)} are computed according to Section 3.2 . If we are interested in the predictive distribution of observation y ~ ∗ \widetilde{y}_{*} , we further convolve q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})) with the likelihood. 3.4 Computational Complexity The computational complexity of our model stems from two components: the SVGP computations, which scale as 𝒪 ​ ( N b ​ P b ​ M 2 + M 3 ) \mathcal{O}(N_{b}P_{b}M^{2}+M^{3}) , and the spectral normalisation operations. Regarding the latter, enforcing Lipschitz constraints via power iteration incurs 𝒪 ​ ( T ​ m ​ n ) \mathcal{O}(Tmn) for an m × n m\times n matrix over T T iterations. However, given the relatively small width ( ∼ 10 \sim 10 ) and depth ( ∼ 5 \sim 5 ) of the RCNNs used in our experiments, the computational overhead of spectral normalisation is often negligible compared to the SVGP computations. 4 Related Work A wide class of MOGP models have been proposed in the literature. Convolved GP framework (Boyle and Frean, 2004 ; Alvarez and Lawrence, 2008 ; Alvarez et al. , 2012 ) employs convolution integrals between smoothing kernels and latent GPs to generate dependent outputs. For isotopic data, the Kronecker product structure between the output and input covariance matrices can be exploited for efficient computation (Bonilla et al. , 2007a ; Stegle et al. , 2011 ; Rakitsch et al. , 2013 ) . In a different vein, Leroy et al. ( 2023 ) shift the focus from covariance to mean functions, modelling multiple tasks through clustering and latent mean processes. Dai et al. ( 2024 ) propose the Graphical MOGP (G-MOGP), which incorporates attention-based graphical models to construct more expressive multi-output priors. Moreno-Muñoz et al. ( 2018 ) extend LMC to heterogeneous prediction tasks, where each output is associated with a (possibly) different likelihood function. Liu et al. ( 2022 ) enhance the LMC by incorporating neural embeddings. By projecting latent GPs into a higher-dimensional feature space, they claim this approach augments the model’s representational capacity. Jeong and Kim ( 2023 ) represent the latent GPs in the LMC as factorial stochastic differential equations for efficiently modelling temporal data. More recently, Rooijakkers et al. ( 2025 ) investigate the sensitivity of MOGPs to misspecification and outliers, proposing robust and conjugate MOGPs. Despite these developments, current methods do not adequately address scalability for high-dimensional output spaces. Bridging this gap is the primary focus of this paper. Further related work is provided in Appendix H . 5 Experiments In this section, we evaluate the proposed T-LVMOGP on several real-world datasets. We benchmark our method against various MOGP baselines, focusing on scalable models such as SV-LMC (Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) , SGPRN (Li et al. , 2020 ) , OILMM (Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) , G-MOGP (Dai et al. , 2024 ) , and GS-LVMOGP (Jiang et al. , 2025 ) . When applicable, Ind-GP (Hensman et al. , 2013 ) is also included for comparison. We provide more details about baseline models in Appendix I , Appendix J and Appendix K . We adopt the ARD-RBF kernel for all models. Performance is assessed using Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) on held-out test data. The wall-clock training time is used to estimate computational efficiency. All reported results represent the mean and standard deviation across 10 random trials, and the best mean values are highlighted in the tables below. A list of acronyms can be found in Appendix A , and further experimental setups are described in Appendix F . 5.1 Electroencephalogram (EEG) Prediction Figure 3 : Predictions of Ind-GP, SV-LMC and T-LVMOGP for F2 electrode with 95 % 95\% confidence region in the EEG experiment. ERA5 The ERA5 dataset (Copernicus Climate Change Service, 2024 ) combines model data with global observations via data assimilation. In this study, we focus on the monthly 2 ​ m 2\,\text{m} air temperature over the United Kingdom. Our dataset comprises 3 , 395 3,395 spatial locations spanning a 30 30 -month period from June 2023 to November 2025. All temperature data are partitioned into training ( 70 % 70\% ) and test ( 30 % 30\% ) sets. The task is to predict the temperature on the test split given the train split. We evaluate using two distinct splitting mechanisms: random and block-wise. For clarity, we present the random split results in this section, while the block-wise split results are detailed in Appendix F.4.1 . Table 5 provides the experimental results. As shown in the table, our model surpasses the baselines in both MSE and NLL. This result is further validated by Figure 4 . Regarding computational efficiency, our method requires less training time than SGPRN, OILMM, and GS-LVMOGP. While G-MOGP and SV-LMC exhibit lower training times than T-LVMOGP, our model outperforms them significantly in predictive performance, measured from MSE and NLL. Consequently, our model achieves the most favourable trade-off between model expressiveness and computational efficiency. In this experiment, we consider a 10x Genomics Visium human prostate cancer dataset (10XGenomics2025, 2025 ) , comprising gene expression counts for 5 , 000 5,000 genes across 4 , 352 4,352 spatial locations. As illustrated in Figure 10 in Appendix F.5 , the data is characterised by high sparsity and significant over-dispersion. To accommodate these characteristics, we employ the Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) likelihood following BinTayyash et al. ( 2021 ) . Details regarding the ZINB likelihood are provided in Appendix E . By formulating spatial spots as inputs and genes as outputs, we obtain a total of 21 , 760 , 000 21,760,000 observations. We implement a random 90 % / 10 % 90\%/10\% train-test split to benchmark T-LVMOGP against GS-LVMOGP, both utilising the ZINB likelihood. The experimental results are summarised in Table 7 . Our model outperforms GS-LVMOGP with Q = 1 , 2 , 3 Q=1,2,3 in terms of MSE, and achieves comparable NLL values to GS-LVMOGP with Q = 2 , 3 Q=2,3 . Although GS-LVMOGP with Q = 1 Q=1 requires the lowest training time, our model surpasses it in terms of both MSE and NLL. ( 2025 ) , tighter variational bounds yield performance gains for single-output GP. Our framework naturally extends this advantage to multi-output settings. As reported in Table 10 and Table 27 in Appendix F.7 , optimising our model with tighter variational bounds results in performance that is comparable to or better than the standard bound. These findings empirically validate the effectiveness of employing tighter variational bounds for multi-output learning within our T-LVMOGP framework. Central to our approach is a Lipschitz-regularised multi-output deep kernel that reformulates MOGPs as scalar GPs defined on a learned embedding space. This transformation enables a direct reuse of scalable inference methods developed for single-output GPs, thereby yielding scalability with respect to the number of outputs. We instantiate the proposed model within the SVGP framework, considering both standard and tighter variational bounds. Future work includes integrating other scalability techniques like structured kernel interpolation (Wilson and Nickisch, 2015 ) and nearest-neighbour approximations (Tran et al. , 2021 ; Wu et al. , 2022 ) . Our model relies on a factorised variational distribution for latent variables, which scales linearly with the number of outputs but limits the capture of posterior output couplings. Future work could address this via structured variational distributions (Mishkin et al. , 2018 ) or amortised inference (Kingma and Welling, 2014 ) . TITLE: Transformed Latent Variable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes

ABSTRACT: Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) provide a principled probabilistic framework for modelling correlated outputs but face scalability bottlenecks when applied to datasets with high-dimensional output spaces. To maintain tractability, existing methods typically resort to restrictive assumptions, such as employing low-rank or sum-of-separable kernels, which can limit expressiveness. We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), a novel framework that scales MOGPs to a massive number of outputs while preserving the capacity to capture meaningful inter-output dependencies. T-LVMOGP constructs a flexible multi-output deep kernel by mapping inputs and output-specific latent variables into an embedding space using a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Combined with stochastic variational inference, our model effectively scales to high-dimensional output settings. Across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with over 10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics data, T-LVMOGP outperforms baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

INTRODUCTION: Gaussian Processes (GPs) offer a principled and flexible Bayesian nonparametric framework for learning unknown functions (Williams and Rasmussen, 2006 ) . Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) extend this framework to model correlations between output functions, proving effective across domains ranging from medical time-series prediction (Cheng et al. , 2020 ) to dynamical system modelling (Tang et al. , 2025 ) . However, standard MOGP models incur a cubic computational cost with respect to the number of outputs P P . This constitutes a bottleneck for high-dimensional applications such as climate modelling and spatial transcriptomics, where P P can easily reach the order of thousands (Bonilla et al. , 2007a ; Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) . Figure 1 : Schematic overview of the proposed T-LVMOGP framework. Inducing points 𝐙 \mathbf{Z} are placed in the embedding space. To mitigate this complexity, existing research largely focuses on imposing structural constraints, potentially sacrificing expressiveness for scalability. For instance, low-rank approaches assume output functions are linear combinations of shared latent functions (Wackernagel, 2003 ; Higdon et al. , 2008 ; Xing et al. , 2016 ; Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) . While computationally efficient, these linear assumptions often lack the expressiveness required to model intricate inter-output dependencies. For tensor-based observations, the outputs can be shaped into a multidimensional array so that the Kronecker and tensor algebra are exploited for efficient computation (Zhe et al. , 2019 ; Li et al. , 2020 ) . However, this approach is predicated on rigid structural assumptions, which are frequently violated in real-world scenarios. Dai et al. ( 2017 ) introduce a latent variable for each output, and construct separable kernels for MOGP. Jiang et al. ( 2025 ) generalise this idea to sum-of-separable kernels, and they apply stochastic variational inference techniques (Hoffman et al. , 2013 ; Hensman et al. , 2013 ) to the output space, facilitating better scalability with respect to an increasing number of outputs. However, their expressivity remains bound by the fixed structural properties of the kernel class. In this work, we propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), illustrated in Figure 1 , a framework that scales MOGPs to large output dimensions while preserving high expressiveness. To this end, we introduce a multi-output deep kernel that captures intricate correlations among outputs without imposing rigid structural constraints. We associate each output with a latent variable, serving as a compact representation (Bonilla et al. , 2007b ) . To ensure high expressiveness, we employ a neural network to map the concatenated input-latent representations into an embedding space, where correlations are defined (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2007 ; Calandra et al. , 2016 ; Wilson et al. , 2016b ) . This architecture enables seamless integration with existing scalable GP frameworks to handle a large output space. We employ the Sparse Variational GP (SVGP) framework (Titsias, 2009 ) , placing inducing points in the embedding space and performing stochastic variational inference following Hensman et al. ( 2013 ) . Furthermore, recently proposed tighter variational bounds (Titsias, 2025 ; Bui et al. , 2025 ) can be readily incorporated into this framework, which potentially enhances predictive performance. While deep kernels provide the requisite flexibility, naive implementations suffer from overfitting (Ober et al. , 2021 ) . In this work, we regularise the neural network via residual connections (He et al. , 2016 ) and spectral normalisation (Miyato et al. , 2018 ) . This encourages the transformation to be Lipschitz continuous, and its distance awareness promotes locally smooth and globally stable mappings. This is critical for the reliable performance of the multi-output deep kernel proposed in this work. Contributions This work makes the following contributions: (1) We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP, a scalable framework for building flexible multi-output deep kernels that scale to high-dimensional outputs while preserving rich cross-output dependencies. (2) To improve generalisation, we adopt a Lipschitz continuous neural network using residual connections and spectral normalisation. (3) We develop a stochastic variational inference procedure within the SVGP framework, supporting mini-batch training over both inputs and outputs. Our formulation naturally accommodates non-Gaussian likelihoods and tighter variational bounds. (4) Empirical experiments demonstrate that our model outperforms MOGP baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

DISCUSSION: Transformed Latent Variable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes: Supplementary Materials Xiaoyu Jiang Xinxing Shi Sokratia Georgaka Magnus Rattray Mauricio A Álvarez Abstract Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) provide a principled probabilistic framework for modelling correlated outputs but face scalability bottlenecks when applied to datasets with high-dimensional output spaces. T-LVMOGP constructs a flexible multi-output deep kernel by mapping inputs and output-specific latent variables into an embedding space using a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Combined with stochastic variational inference, our model effectively scales to high-dimensional output settings. Across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with over 10 , 000 10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics data, T-LVMOGP outperforms baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. LIMITATIONS: In the current formulation, we employ a mean-field approximation for the variational distribution of the latent variables, factorising q ​ ( 𝐇 ) q(\mathbf{H}) across outputs to ensure computational efficiency. While this enhances scalability, it may limit posterior expressiveness by ignoring potential coupling among outputs. Future work could address this trade-off by adopting structured variational distributions (Mishkin et al. , 2018 ; Tomczak et al. , 2020 ) , which capture complex dependencies without incurring prohibitive computational costs. Furthermore, although the parameter complexity of q ​ ( 𝐇 ) q(\mathbf{H}) scales linearly with the number of outputs P P , future work could investigate imposing additional constraints within the latent space, such as amortisation (Kingma and Welling, 2014 ) . Such constraints would reduce the parameterisation required for estimation, thereby further improving scalability for high-dimensional output spaces.

CONCLUSION: In this work, we introduce a new MOGP framework that alleviates the computational bottleneck induced by high-dimensional outputs while retaining sufficient modelling capacity to capture rich inter-output dependencies. Central to our approach is a Lipschitz-regularised multi-output deep kernel that reformulates MOGPs as scalar GPs defined on a learned embedding space. This transformation enables a direct reuse of scalable inference methods developed for single-output GPs, thereby yielding scalability with respect to the number of outputs. We instantiate the proposed model within the SVGP framework, considering both standard and tighter variational bounds. Future work includes integrating other scalability techniques like structured kernel interpolation (Wilson and Nickisch, 2015 ) and nearest-neighbour approximations (Tran et al. , 2021 ; Wu et al. , 2022 ) . Our model relies on a factorised variational distribution for latent variables, which scales linearly with the number of outputs but limits the capture of posterior output couplings. Future work could address this via structured variational distributions (Mishkin et al. , 2018 ) or amortised inference (Kingma and Welling, 2014 ) .

In this section, we relate the kernel structure of T-LVMOGP to that of prior Latent Variable MOGP models (Dai et al. , 2017 ; Jiang et al. , 2025 ) . We focus on the (ARD)-RBF kernels used throughout the paper.

To ensure a fair comparison, we implement all models, including the proposed T-LVMOGP and the baselines (Ind-GP, SV-LMC, SGPRN, OILMM, G-MOGP, and GS-LVMOGP), using the PyTorch (Paszke et al. , 2019 ) and GPyTorch (Gardner et al. , 2018 ) frameworks. Experiments are conducted on a high-performance computing cluster utilising NVIDIA HGX A100-SXM4 or NVIDIA L40S-SXM4 GPUs, with all computations performed in double precision ( Float64 ). For computational efficiency assessment, training times are benchmarked on an NVIDIA RTX-5090 GPU. Throughout this work, we employ the ARD-RBF kernel as the base kernel of T-LVMOGP for simplicity; and J = 1 J=1 Monte Carlo sample is adopted for model training. In terms of tighter variational bounds, the correction term Δ \Delta given by Equation 1 is evaluated conditionally on sampled 𝐡 p ( j ) \mathbf{h}_{p}^{(j)} . We draw input-output pairs from Ω \Omega and scale the mini-batch expected log-likelihood term in ℒ ~ 3 \widetilde{\mathcal{L}}_{3} by N train / ( N ​ P ) N_{\text{train}}/(NP) , where N train < N ​ P N_{\text{train}}<NP denotes the total number of observed training data, to ensure an unbiased weight for the expected log-likelihood term. We choose S = 20 S=20 samples for latent variables when making predictions on the test set. The NLL is computed as follows: NLL = − log ⁡ q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐱 ∗ ) = − log ​ ∫ q ​ ( 𝐡 p ∗ ) ​ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 𝐡 p ∗ ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) \displaystyle=-\log q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{x}_{*})=-\log\int q(\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}})q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,d\mathbf{h}_{p_{*}}\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}) ≈ − log ⁡ 1 S ​ ∑ s ∫ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) \displaystyle\approx-\log\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s}\int q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}) = log ⁡ S − logsumexp s = 1 , … , S [ log ⁡ ∫ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ​ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ) ​ 𝑑 f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ⏟ q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ] . \displaystyle=\log S-\operatorname*{logsumexp}_{s=1,...,S}\left[\log\underbrace{\int q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})p(y_{*}\mid f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*}))\,df_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})}_{q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})}\right]. For the Gaussian likelihood, the integral for q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}) can be analytically solved. For non-Gaussian likelihoods, we approximate it by plugging in the mean value of q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}) , i.e. q ​ ( y ∗ ∣ 𝐡 p ∗ s ) ≈ p ​ ( y ∗ ∣ f mean* ) , where ​ f mean* ​ represents the mean value of ​ q ​ ( f p ∗ ​ ( 𝐱 ∗ ) ∣ 𝐱 ∗ , 𝐡 p ∗ s ) . q(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}})\approx p(y_{*}\mid f_{\text{mean*}}),\;\text{where }f_{\text{mean*}}\text{ represents the mean value of }q(f_{p_{*}}(\mathbf{x}_{*})\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{h}^{s}_{p_{*}}).

There are more works about MOGPs in the literature. Yu et al. ( 2005 ) exploit the equivalence between parametric linear models and GPs for multi-task learning in a hierarchical Bayesian framework. Semiparametric Latent Factor Models (SLFM) can be viewed as a special case of LMC (Teh et al. , 2005 ) . Latent Force Models (LFMs) (Alvarez et al. , 2009 ) induce dependencies by passing latent GP forces through mechanistic differential equations, yielding physically interpretable multi-output kernels. Melkumyan and Ramos ( 2011 ) develop multi-task covariance functions by combining stationary kernels via Fourier arguments under the process convolution framework. Collaborative MOGP (Nguyen and Bonilla, 2014 ) represents output functions as a combination of two elements: projections from latent GPs shared across all outputs and individual GPs capturing the output-specific residuals. Kaiser et al. ( 2018 ) propose a hierarchical MOGP model, where Convolution Process layers are embedded in the middle of Deep Gaussian Processes (DGP) (Damianou and Lawrence, 2013 ) for explicitly modelling output dependencies. To enhance expressivity in multichannel signal modelling, the Multi-Output Spectral Mixture kernel (MOSM) (Parra and Tobar, 2017 ; Altamirano and Tobar, 2022 ) is introduced, which is effective for modelling phase differences and delays among outputs. García-Hinde et al. ( 2022 ) reformulate the MOGP problem as a sequence of conditioned univariate GPs by augmenting the input space with previous output values—a strategy similarly adopted in autoregressive GP modelling (Requeima et al. , 2019 ) . While MOGP literature predominantly focuses on modelling output correlations through complex covariance structures, Fortuin et al. ( 2019 ) demonstrate that meta-learning deep mean functions can be a competitive strategy for knowledge transfer in low-data regimes. In terms of applications and specific extensions, Zhao and Sun ( 2016 ) propose a convolved MOGP model for complex dynamical systems such as motion capture, traffic flow and robot inverse dynamics. Yousefi et al. ( 2019 ) construct an MOGP model based on LMC for modelling data points that have been aggregated at different input scales. To improve heterogeneous modelling of MOGP, Giraldo and Alvarez ( 2021 ) propose a fully natural gradient scheme applicable for both LMC and process convolution. López-Lopera et al. ( 2022 ) apply an MOGP model with a separable kernel to surrogate modelling of coastal flood hazard assessment. Yoon et al. ( 2022 ) introduce a specialised variant of LMC that decomposes input effects on outputs into components shared across or specific to tasks and samples. Ma and Álvarez ( 2023 ) introduce a multi-output multi-class classification model based on LMC. Ma et al. ( 2023 ) extend LV-MOGP for modelling hierarchical datasets, where several replicates are observed for each output.

We provide a comparative analysis of the baseline models (SV-LMC (Van der Wilk et al. , 2020 ) , SGPRN (Li et al. , 2020 ) , OILMM (Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) , G-MOGP (Dai et al. , 2024 ) , and GS-LVMOGP (Jiang et al. , 2025 ) ), focusing on their respective model sizes and computational costs based on our implementation. Notably, in the following evaluation, kernel parameters are excluded from the parameter enumeration.

In this section, we present a detailed review of the two scalable MOGP models adopted as baselines in this paper—namely, SGPRN (Li et al. , 2020 ) and OILMM (Bruinsma et al. , 2020 ) —to provide interested readers with additional background and context.

This section provides a comparison between the T-LVMOGP and baseline models from a methodological perspective.

sections.discussion: By far the most common way to estimate an expected loss in machine learning is to draw samples, compute the loss on each one, and take the empirical average. However, sampling is not necessarily optimal. Given an MLP at initialization, we show how to estimate its expected output over Gaussian inputs without running samples through the network at all. Instead, we produce approximate representations of the distributions of activations at each layer, leveraging tools such as cumulants and Hermite expansions. We show both theoretically and empirically that for sufficiently wide networks, our estimator achieves a target mean squared error using substantially fewer FLOPs than Monte Carlo sampling. We find moreover that our methods perform particularly well at estimating the probabilities of rare events, and additionally demonstrate how they can be used for model training. Together, these findings suggest a path to producing models with a greatly reduced probability of catastrophic tail risks.
